title: Implement real XINFO CONSUMERS pending count and idle/inactive time
description:
## Problem

fr-store/src/lib.rs line 5796: xinfo_consumers returns Vec<Vec<u8>> (just consumer names).
fr-command/src/lib.rs lines 4169-4179: stream_consumer_info_to_frame hardcodes pending=0, idle=0, inactive=0.

## What Redis Returns

XINFO CONSUMERS key group returns per-consumer:
- name: consumer name
- pending: number of PEL entries owned by this consumer
- idle: milliseconds since last interaction (XREADGROUP/XCLAIM/XACK)
- inactive: milliseconds since last successful XREADGROUP (Redis 7.2+)

## Implementation Plan

1. Change Store::xinfo_consumers to return Vec<(Vec<u8>, usize, u64, u64)> — (name, pending_count, last_interaction_ms, last_read_ms)
2. Compute pending_count by scanning the PEL and counting entries where consumer matches
3. Track last_interaction_ms per consumer in StreamGroupState (update on XREADGROUP, XCLAIM, XACK)
4. Track last_read_ms per consumer (update only on XREADGROUP with >)
5. Update stream_consumer_info_to_frame to use real values

## Files to Modify

- crates/fr-store/src/lib.rs: StreamGroupState consumer tracking, xinfo_consumers
- crates/fr-command/src/lib.rs: stream_consumer_info_to_frame
close_reason: XINFO CONSUMERS now returns real pending count and idle time computed from PEL, not hardcoded zeros

title: [code-review] CONFIG SET accepts invalid newly-added static keys
description: Review of 3313cbe found that adding upstream CONFIG GET-only static keys also made the generic CONFIG SET static-override fallback accept them. Repro on current main: CONFIG SET tls-cluster maybe, CONFIG SET cluster-port 1, or CONFIG SET syslog-enabled yes can return OK even though upstream Redis declares tls-cluster as bool yes/no and cluster-port/syslog-enabled as immutable. Acceptance: newly-added static keys retain CONFIG GET shape parity but CONFIG SET rejects immutable keys and validates Redis bool/int/enum types before storing overrides.
close_reason: Duplicate of frankenredis-3hj8; leaving active fix with the peer-owned review bead

title: fr-conformance: 2 stale memory_unknown_subcmd fixtures pin pre-wlwb verbose wording, mask byte-equal upstream parity
description:
core_server.json has TWO duplicate cases pinning the OLD verbose MEMORY unknown-subcommand wording:

  memory_unknown_subcmd_nosuch (line 2286):
    expects: ERR Unknown subcommand or wrong number of arguments for 'nosuch'. Try MEMORY HELP.
  memory_unknown_subcmd_duplicate_block (line 2736):
    expects: ERR Unknown subcommand or wrong number of arguments for 'nosuch'. Try MEMORY HELP.

But fr now emits the upstream-aligned shorter wording (verified by frankenredis-wlwb migration):

  upstream + fr both: ERR unknown subcommand 'NOSUCH'. Try MEMORY HELP.

Differences vs the stale expected:
  - lowercase 'unknown' (was 'Unknown')
  - uppercase 'NOSUCH' preserves user-typed casing (was lowercase 'nosuch')
  - shorter form drops 'or wrong number of arguments for'

These two fixture cases have been red on main since the wlwb migration landed; my 2e40e29 conformance run surfaced them as part of a 7-case pre-existing failure block.

Acceptance: both fixture cases reflect the upstream wording, the
core_server_conformance suite shrinks the pre-existing failure list by 2.

Reference: /testing-conformance-harnesses fixture cleanup on 2026-05-04.
notes: Updated both stale fixture cases (memory_unknown_subcmd_nosuch + memory_unknown_subcmd_duplicate_block) in core_server.json to expect the upstream-aligned wording 'ERR unknown subcommand NOSUCH. Try MEMORY HELP.' (lowercase 'unknown', preserves user-typed 'NOSUCH' casing, drops 'or wrong number of arguments for'). Conformance pass count goes 275 → 277 (the 2 corrected cases now pass).

title: COMMAND DOCS subcommand iteration order is alphabetical, vendored uses Redis siphash dict order
description: fr's command_docs_subcommands iterates SUBCOMMAND_TABLE entries in alphabetical order (table is sorted alphabetically), while vendored Redis 7.2.4 emits subcommands in dictGetIterator order, which is determined by Redis's siphash hashing. Achieving byte-equivalent ordering would require either: (a) replicating siphash with the same seed Redis uses, or (b) hardcoding the observed order per parent at build time. Discovered while working frankenredis-bpf4q. Cosmetic only — structure is correct, only ordering differs.
close_reason: Wontfix — verified that vendored's subcommand iteration order is NON-DETERMINISTIC across restarts. Redis 7.2.4 initializes the dict siphash seed from random at startup (dictSetHashFunctionSeed in dict.c). Captured two CLUSTER subcommand orderings from two consecutive vendored restarts: run 1 started 'cluster|saveconfig/reset/forget/help/setslot...', run 2 started 'cluster|links/slots/set-config-epoch/shards/delslots...'. Neither is canonical, so there is no stable byte-equivalent target to match. fr's alphabetical order is at least deterministic. No further action needed.

title: Expand fuzz corpus with diverse seed inputs
notes: Expanded: RDB 3→14 seeds, AOF 5→14 seeds, DUMP 2→5 seeds covering all data types

title: CONFIG GET save defaults to empty — vendored defaults to '3600 1 300 100 60 10000' (Redis's standard auto-snapshot policy)
description:
Differential probe vs vendored 7.2.4 freshly-started server:
ven CONFIG GET save → '3600 1 300 100 60 10000'
fr  CONFIG GET save → ''

Per upstream config.c::initServerConfig + DEFAULT_SAVE_PARAMS, fresh Redis 7.2 boots with the trio (3600s, 1 change), (300s, 100 changes), (60s, 10000 changes). fr boots with empty save policy, so an unmodified config never triggers auto-BGSAVE. Affects scripted setups that rely on the default snapshot cadence — they'd silently lose data without a CONFIG SET save.

Fix: initialize Server.save_params (or whatever the live store of save points is) with the upstream default '3600 1 300 100 60 10000' on Store::new(), unless --save '' or appendonly mode is enabled per the redis.conf overrides.
close_reason: Updated CONFIG_DEFAULTS for both fr-runtime (line 378) and fr-command (line 15165) to '3600 1 300 100 60 10000', matching upstream config.c CONFIG_DEFAULT_SAVE_PARAMS. Updated config_get_save fixture to match. Also fixed two replication tests (replication_sync_alias_triggers_fullresync, replication_psync_accepts_trailing_arguments_like_legacy_redis) that were asserting the all-zeros placeholder replid post-frankenredis-d1505 — they now read the runtime's actual replid for the FULLRESYNC reply assertion.

title: [HIGH] COPY src==dst returns 0/1 instead of error + RESTORE busy-key error precedence
description: Surfaced by live_redis_core_copy_matches_runtime: copy_src_eq_dst_no_replace + copy_src_eq_dst_with_replace — upstream 'ERR source and destination objects are the same', ours returns Integer(0)/Integer(1) as if copy succeeded. restore_bad_payload_existing_key — our error-check order gives 'DUMP payload version or checksum are wrong' before BUSYKEY; upstream checks existence first. Fix in fr-command COPY (add src-vs-dst guard) + RESTORE (move BUSYKEY check before payload validation).
close_reason: Already resolved: live_redis_core_copy_matches_runtime passes 100% under STRICT mode (COPY src==dst and RESTORE BUSYKEY precedence fixes landed previously).

title: CONFIG SET validation gaps batch: maxmemory-clients (no validation); active-defrag-ignore-bytes (accepts 0); replica/slave-priority (wrong wording)
description:
Differential probe vs vendored 16380:

(1) maxmemory-clients accepts arbitrary values:
  CONFIG SET maxmemory-clients -1
    vendored: ERR ... argument must be a memory or percent value
    fr      : OK
  CONFIG SET maxmemory-clients bogus
    same divergence

  Upstream config.c declares maxmemory-clients with a special validator that accepts memory sizes (1g, 100mb) OR percentages (50%). fr has no validator and silently stores garbage.

(2) active-defrag-ignore-bytes accepts 0 (must be >= 1):
  CONFIG SET active-defrag-ignore-bytes 0
    vendored: ERR ... argument must be between 1 and 9223372036854775807 inclusive
    fr      : OK

  Upstream declares it as INTEGER_CONFIG with inclusive range [1, INT64_MAX], not [0, INT64_MAX]. fr accepts 0.

(3) replica-priority / slave-priority emit non-standard wording:
  CONFIG SET replica-priority -1
    vendored: ERR ... argument must be between 0 and 2147483647 inclusive
    fr      : ERR Invalid argument for CONFIG SET 'replica-priority'

  CONFIG SET replica-priority bogus
    vendored: ERR ... argument couldn't be parsed into an integer
    fr      : ERR value is not an integer or out of range

  Same template miss as ngoap/qqt06/pgl7g. Should route through config_set_failed with the table-level 'argument must be ...' / 'argument couldn't be parsed ...' wording.

  slave-priority is the legacy alias and currently has the same wrong wording.

Fix: in fr-runtime's handle_config_set:
- Add maxmemory-clients branch with parse_memory_or_percent helper (or reuse existing maxmemory parse + percent check).
- Tighten active-defrag-ignore-bytes lower bound from 0 to 1.
- Replace replica-priority's existing 'Invalid argument' wording with config_set_failed; also handle the slave-priority alias.
close_reason: Bundled fix for three CONFIG SET validation gaps in fr-runtime/src/lib.rs: (1) replica-priority/slave-priority: replaced the non-standard 'Invalid argument' wording (and the generic 'value is not an integer' parse error fallthrough) with config_set_failed table-level wording 'argument must be between 0 and 2147483647 inclusive' / 'argument couldn't be parsed into an integer'; also tightened the upper bound to i32::MAX. (2) active-defrag-ignore-bytes: added an explicit >0 check after parse_memory_size_arg (upstream INTEGER_CONFIG range is [1, INT64_MAX], not [0, INT64_MAX]); routed the rejection through config_set_failed with the upstream wording. (3) maxmemory-clients: added a dedicated handler that accepts memory sizes (1g, 100mb) OR percentages (50%); invalid forms surface 'argument must be a memory or percent value'. Pinned via three new tests covering bound/parse error wording, valid acceptance, and percent forms. Live differential probe vs vendored 16380 confirms identical replies for all 6 cases (maxmemory-clients -1/50%, active-defrag-ignore-bytes 0, replica-priority -1/bogus, slave-priority -1). Workspace clippy clean under -D warnings.

title: [HIGH] fr-persist::decode_rdb: support upstream compact encoding type tags 11/16/17/18/20
description:
Surfaced by br-frankenredis-8w1e RDB corpus harvest test (live_redis_rdb_corpus_decodes_cleanly): when upstream encodes small data structures, it uses compact-form RDB type bytes that fr-persist::decode_rdb does not recognize today. Match table:

* RDB_TYPE_SET_INTSET (11) — all-integer set, intset payload
* RDB_TYPE_HASH_LISTPACK (16) — small hash, listpack payload
* RDB_TYPE_ZSET_LISTPACK (17) — small zset, listpack payload
* RDB_TYPE_LIST_QUICKLIST_2 (18) — list, quicklist of listpacks (upstream ALWAYS uses this for lists, regardless of size)
* RDB_TYPE_SET_LISTPACK (20) — small set, listpack payload

Without these, fr-persist returns InvalidFrame on any upstream-produced dump.rdb that contains a list of any size, OR small sets/hashes/zsets that haven't been pinned past their listpack thresholds. The br-frankenredis-8w1e test pins thresholds to 0 to bypass; lifting that pin requires implementing these decoders.

Listpack and intset decoders already exist in fr-store (used for DUMP/RESTORE). The fix is mostly wiring fr-store helpers into fr-persist::decode_rdb, plus a quicklist envelope decoder for type 18.

Acceptance: re-enable compact encoding pins in live_redis_rdb_corpus_decodes_cleanly (drop the CONFIG SET *=0 block) and add the corresponding shapes back to expected_keys; harvest runs through with all 11+ shapes decoding cleanly.
close_reason: Shipped in ec6a274: fr-persist::decode_rdb now handles upstream compact encoding tags RDB_TYPE_SET_INTSET (11), RDB_TYPE_HASH_LISTPACK (16), RDB_TYPE_ZSET_LISTPACK (17), RDB_TYPE_LIST_QUICKLIST_2 (18), RDB_TYPE_SET_LISTPACK (20). New decode_intset_members helper covers 16/32/64-bit widths; listpack decoders reuse fr_persist::listpack::decode_listpack; quicklist accepts both PLAIN (container=1) and PACKED (container=2) nodes. Test coverage: 10 new fr-persist unit tests with hand-built fixtures + 5 malformed-input rejection cases; the live_redis_rdb_corpus_decodes_cleanly conformance test (br-frankenredis-8w1e) drops its threshold-pinning workaround and now exercises 11 shapes covering every type tag fr-persist accepts against a real upstream-produced dump.rdb. Validated via rch cargo check/test/clippy: 130 fr-persist tests, 297 fr-store tests, 186 fr-conformance lib tests, all green; clippy -D warnings clean.

title: COMMAND_TABLE arity divergences for ZRANK/ZREVRANK/ZREVRANGE/XAUTOCLAIM (handlers correct, table metadata stale)
description:
Four COMMAND_TABLE entries report arities that don't match upstream commands.def
(handler logic is already correct; only `COMMAND INFO <cmd>` metadata diverges).

Probe vs vendored Redis 7.2.4:
  COMMAND INFO ZRANK      -> upstream: -3   fr: 3
  COMMAND INFO ZREVRANK   -> upstream: -3   fr: 3
  COMMAND INFO ZREVRANGE  -> upstream: -4   fr: 4
  COMMAND INFO XAUTOCLAIM -> upstream: -6   fr: -7

ZRANK/ZREVRANK gained an optional WITHSCORE modifier in Redis 7.2 (3 or 4 args),
ZREVRANGE has [WITHSCORES] (4 or 5 args), and XAUTOCLAIM's minimum is
argc=6 (key group consumer min-idle-time start) - fr's table required argc>=7,
advertising a stricter contract than the handler actually enforces.

Fix: switch the COMMAND_TABLE entries to the upstream-declared arities.

title: Wire CONFIG SET maxclients to fr-server connection acceptance
description:
## Problem

fr-server/src/main.rs line 33 defines `const MAX_CLIENTS: usize = 10_000` and uses it at line 499 to reject new connections. CONFIG SET maxclients updates the config table in fr-runtime but never propagates to fr-server's actual enforcement.

## What Redis Does

Redis's CONFIG SET maxclients immediately changes the rlimit and connection acceptance threshold. New connections beyond the limit get an error response and are closed.

## Implementation Plan

1. Add a `max_clients` field to `ServerState` in fr-runtime (initialized to 10000)
2. In `handle_config_set`, when maxclients is set, update `self.server.max_clients`
3. In fr-server's main loop, read `runtime.server.max_clients` (or expose via a getter) instead of the const
4. Also update `store.server_maxclients` so INFO reports the correct value
5. Remove the `MAX_CLIENTS` const from fr-server

## Files to Modify

- `crates/fr-runtime/src/lib.rs`: Add field to ServerState, handle in config_set
- `crates/fr-server/src/main.rs`: Replace const with runtime read
- `crates/fr-store/src/lib.rs`: Already has server_maxclients field (added in f8c5a8a)

## Testing

- Unit test: CONFIG SET maxclients 100 → CONFIG GET maxclients returns 100
- Unit test: INFO clients section reports the new maxclients value
- Integration: verify connection rejection beyond the configured limit (may need fr-conformance fixture)
close_reason: maxclients wired: ServerState.max_clients + CONFIG SET/GET + fr-server reads runtime value

title: ZRANGE: duplicate REV/BYSCORE/BYLEX errors + LIMIT count=-1 sentinel allowed in rank mode
description: (1) Duplicate REV, BYSCORE, BYLEX silently accepted. Upstream t_zset.c::zrangeGenericCommand only matches REV when direction==AUTO and BYLEX/BYSCORE when rangetype==AUTO; the second occurrence falls through to addReplyErrorObject(c, shared.syntaxerr) (2) LIMIT-without-BYSCORE/BYLEX rejected ALL count values; upstream's check is opt_limit != -1 so LIMIT offset -1 (sentinel for 'no limit count') is allowed even in rank mode

title: fr-command FCALL/FCALL_RO non-integer numkeys returns generic 'value is not an integer' instead of upstream's 'Bad number of keys provided'
close_reason:
FCALL and FCALL_RO now emit upstream's bespoke 'Bad number of keys provided' wording for non-integer numkeys, instead of the EVAL-shaped 'value is not an integer or out of range'.

CHANGE (crates/fr-command/src/lib.rs):
- Added fcall_map_eval_arg_error helper that converts CommandError::InvalidInteger into CommandError::Custom('ERR Bad number of keys provided'). Other CommandError variants pass through unchanged.
- Wrapped both call sites of parse_eval_args inside fcall_cmd (the script-nesting pre-validation path and the main parse path) with .map_err(fcall_map_eval_arg_error).

This mirrors upstream functions.c::fcallCommandGeneric line 638-641 which uses `getLongLongFromObject` (no _OrReply suffix) and emits a bespoke error string distinct from EVAL's getLongLongFromObjectOrReply path. The 'Number of keys can't be negative' / 'can't be greater than number of args' wordings already match upstream and don't need remapping.

Regression test fcall_non_integer_numkeys_uses_upstream_specific_wording loads a trivial function via FUNCTION LOAD then probes FCALL with NOTANINT numkeys. (The test exercises FCALL only; FCALL_RO's write-flag pre-check fires before parse_eval_args because fr does not yet surface the Lua no-writes flag — separate gap, out of scope.)

Verification:
- cargo test -p fr-command --lib fcall → 3/3 pass.
- cargo clippy -p fr-command --lib --no-deps -- -D warnings → clean.

[LOW] severity. Wire-format conformance gap.

title: fr-command FUNCTION STATS returns flat Array even in RESP3 — same shape pattern as i40x2/e4njz/udl3y
description:
/testing-conformance-harnesses follow-up to udl3y/e4njz/i40x2:

  FUNCTION STATS (RESP3, with HELLO 3)
    upstream: nested Map shape — outer { running_script: nil, engines: { LUA: { libraries_count: 0, functions_count: 0 } } }
    fr      : flat Array — every key/value/sub-array on its own RESP element

Source: crates/fr-command/src/lib.rs:8729-8752 — function_cmd's STATS branch builds a flat 4-element Array (running_script, value, engines, value) without checking dispatch_client_ctx.resp_protocol_version. Same architectural drift as the three already-fixed handlers.

Upstream functions.c::functionStatsCommand:
  - addReplyMapLen(c, 2) for outer
  - addReplyMapLen(c, n_engines) for engines value
  - addReplyMapLen(c, 2) for each engine's per-engine stats (libraries_count, functions_count)

So the RESP3 shape is doubly-nested Map. RESP2 stays flat Array (matches current).

Fix: emit RespFrame::Map with two pairs (running_script, nil) and (engines, RespFrame::Map of one pair (LUA, RespFrame::Map of (libraries_count, X) (functions_count, Y))) under RESP3; keep the flat-Array path under RESP2.
close_reason: fr-command FUNCTION STATS now branches on store.dispatch_client_ctx.resp_protocol_version: emits nested RespFrame::Map under RESP3 (outer 2 entries running_script + engines, engines is Map of engine→Map, per-engine Map carries libraries_count + functions_count) — mirroring upstream functions.c::functionStatsCommand's doubly-nested addReplyMapLen. RESP2 stays the existing flat Array. Two new pin tests: function_stats_under_resp3_returns_nested_map_shape (asserts the outer Map structure + per-engine Map shape with key order) and function_stats_under_resp2_stays_flat_array. Existing function_stats_rejects_extra_args + 1 other still pass; clippy clean. Same shape pattern as i40x2 (TRACKINGINFO), e4njz (ACL GETUSER), udl3y (MEMORY STATS).

title: redis parity: fr-command wrong-subcommand arity wording should use command text
description:
Evidence: vendored Redis returns command wording for CONFIG RESETSTAT, OBJECT FREQ/IDLETIME, CLIENT GETREDIR/PAUSE, ACL SETUSER/HELP/WHOAMI, XGROUP CREATE, and XINFO STREAM arity failures. Local fr-command still special-cases WrongSubcommandArity::to_resp to emit subcommand wording for several families, and stale unit expectations in lib.rs/lua_eval.rs preserved the wrong oracle text.

Current gap: crates/fr-command/src/lib.rs mismatched the oracle on wrong-subcommand arity text, leaving cargo test -p fr-command red.

Real impl path: simplify WrongSubcommandArity formatting to Redis command wording and refresh the stale unit expectations in crates/fr-command/src/lib.rs and crates/fr-command/src/lua_eval.rs.
close_reason: Completed

title: redis parity: CLIENT NO-EVICT/NO-TOUCH/SETINFO/GETREDIR/TRACKINGINFO should reject script context
description: Vendored Redis marks CLIENT NO-EVICT, CLIENT NO-TOUCH, CLIENT SETINFO, CLIENT GETREDIR, and CLIENT TRACKINGINFO as NOSCRIPT in legacy_redis_code/redis/src/commands/client-no-evict.json, client-no-touch.json, client-setinfo.json, client-getredir.json, and client-trackinginfo.json. The client_cmd branches at crates/fr-command/src/lib.rs:11133, 11144, 11328, and 11335 currently execute without any script_nesting_level guard. Add Redis-compatible rejection after arity validation for each subcommand and cover them from Lua.
close_reason: Completed

title: Refresh p2c_006 WAIT/WAITAOF conformance vectors
close_reason: Refreshed p2c_006 WAIT/WAITAOF conformance expectations to match runtime and Redis semantics

title: fr-command BGSAVE / SHUTDOWN noscript guard placed after handler-level option validation; scripted bad-args surface SyntaxError instead of NOSCRIPT
description:
Differential probe vs upstream Redis 7.2.4: commands.def at legacy_redis_code/redis/src/commands.def:10772 (BGSAVE) and :10793 (SHUTDOWN) carry CMD_NOSCRIPT. Upstream processCommand checks command flags AFTER table-arity but BEFORE the command proc runs, so any handler-level argument validation must come AFTER noscript rejection.

fr-command divergences:

1. BGSAVE at crates/fr-command/src/lib.rs:15971-15991 — noscript guard at line 15982 fires AFTER the application-level extra-arg check (argv.len() > 2 → SyntaxError) and the SCHEDULE-mode validation (lines 15975-15980). Inside a script, BGSAVE EXTRA EXTRA returns SyntaxError instead of 'ERR This Redis command is not allowed from script'. Upstream rdb.c::bgsaveCommand:3639-3651 only enters argument parsing AFTER processCommand has already rejected scripted callers via CMD_NOSCRIPT.

2. SHUTDOWN at crates/fr-command/src/lib.rs:17347-17376 — noscript guard at line 17369 fires AFTER the option-parsing for-loop (lines 17350-17365) and the flag-conflict check (lines 17366-17368). Inside a script, SHUTDOWN BAD returns SyntaxError instead of NOSCRIPT.

Note: LASTSAVE has CMD_LOADING|CMD_STALE|CMD_FAST in commands.def:10781 — NO CMD_NOSCRIPT — so its missing noscript guard is by design and not in scope here.

Fix: For each handler, move the 'if store.script_nesting_level >= 1 { return Err(script_noscript_command_error()); }' block to immediately after the function entry (before any handler-level option/argument validation). Mirrors prior fixes in this family (script_cmd, function_cmd, client_cmd, eval/evalsha/fcall, replicaof, swapdb, save/bgrewriteaof which were already correctly ordered).

Acceptance: scripted 'BGSAVE EXTRA EXTRA' and 'SHUTDOWN BAD' both return NOSCRIPT; non-scripted callers continue to return SyntaxError as before. Test renames mirror the pattern: ..._after_validation → ..._after_arity_check.
close_reason: done

title: OBJECT/TYPE lookups should not touch LRU
description: Redis uses LOOKUP_NOTOUCH for TYPE and OBJECT subcommands. Store::value_type/object_encoding/object_idletime were touching LRU after keyspace lookup, which mutates last_access_ms. Update them to record keyspace stats without touching LRU and add regression tests.
close_reason: TYPE/OBJECT lookups record stats without touching LRU

title: fr-command COMMAND DOCS omits 'history' field — every command's per-version changelog is missing from the docs reply
description:
/testing-conformance-harnesses + /porting-to-rust follow-up to f39s3:

Differential probe vs vendored Redis 7.2.4:

  COMMAND DOCS SET (excerpt)
    upstream: ...complexity\\nO(1)\\nhistory\\n2.6.12\\nAdded the EX, PX, NX and XX options.\\n6.0.0\\nAdded the KEEPTTL option.\\n...
    fr      : ...(no history field at all)...

f39s3 wired summary/complexity/since from upstream JSON but skipped 'history' (a list of [version, message] pairs declared in legacy_redis_code/redis/src/commands/*.json — e.g. SET has 4 history entries, INCR has 1, etc.). Clients that pretty-print COMMAND DOCS (Redis Insight, redis-cli --help, monitoring dashboards) currently drop the per-version changelog for every fr command.

Fix: extend build.rs to harvest a parallel UPSTREAM_COMMAND_DOCS_HISTORY: &[(name, &[(version, message)])] table; have command_docs_entry emit a 'history' field after 'complexity' (matching upstream emission order in t_string.c::commandDocsCommand) when the table has a non-empty entry. Skip when the upstream JSON omits history (most commands).
close_reason: Extended fr-command/build.rs to harvest the 'history' field from legacy_redis_code/redis/src/commands/*.json — emits UPSTREAM_COMMAND_DOCS_HISTORY: &[(name, &[(version, message)])] alongside the existing summary/complexity/since table. command_docs_entry binary-searches the table and emits a 'history' field (between 'complexity' and 'arguments', matching upstream t_string.c::commandDocsCommand emission order) for commands that declared at least one entry; commands without history (like GET) skip the field. Two pin tests: command_docs_emits_upstream_history_for_set asserts SET's 4 entries + first/last byte-for-byte; command_docs_omits_history_for_commands_without_upstream_entries pins GET has no history field. clippy clean; full conformance suite 192/192 pass.

title: fr-command DEBUG OBJECT hardcodes refcount:1; should reuse Store::object_refcount() for shared-integer parity
description:
REVIEW MODE Tick 14 finding via /modes-of-reasoning-project-analysis. DEBUG OBJECT shares the same refcount semantics as OBJECT REFCOUNT (fixed in ljrt6) but still emits a hardcoded value.

LOCATION (crates/fr-command/src/lib.rs lines 16889-16891):
  let debug_info = format!(
      'Value at:0x0 refcount:1 encoding:{encoding} serializedlength:{serialized_len} lru:{idle_secs} lru_seconds_idle:{idle_secs} hexpired_fields:{hexpired_fields}'
  );

UPSTREAM (legacy_redis_code/redis/src/debug.c::debugCommand line 648-654):
  addReplyStatusFormat(c,
      'Value at:%p refcount:%d '
      'encoding:%s serializedlength:%zu '
      'lru:%d lru_seconds_idle:%llu%s',
      (void*)val, val->refcount, ...);

So upstream emits  which is INT_MAX (= 2147483647) for shared integer objects in [0, 10000), and 1 otherwise. Same rule fr already implements via Store::object_refcount() (frankenredis-ljrt6) for the OBJECT REFCOUNT command.

Severity: [LOW]. Same observability divergence as ljrt6 — affects refcount-introspecting tools that use DEBUG OBJECT instead of OBJECT REFCOUNT (some debug scripts query the former for the full encoding/lru bundle).

Reproducer:
  redis-cli> SET k 5
  redis-cli> DEBUG OBJECT k
  upstream: Value at:0x... refcount:2147483647 encoding:int ...
  fr (current): Value at:0x0 refcount:1 encoding:int ...

Fix:
1. Replace hardcoded 'refcount:1' with the live value from Store::object_refcount().
2. Update existing debug_object_reports_live_metadata test to seed an integer key for the shared-int case.
3. Add regression test for the integer case.

Note: DEBUG OBJECT also emits BulkString instead of upstream's SimpleString (addReplyStatusFormat → +...\r\n) — separate fix, deferred to keep this tick tight.
close_reason: fr-command DEBUG OBJECT now reuses Store::object_refcount() helper introduced in ljrt6 instead of hardcoding refcount:1. Mirrors upstream debug.c::debugCommand which emits val->refcount — INT_MAX (i32::MAX = 2147483647) for shared integer objects in [0, 10000) under the default maxmemory_policy, 1 otherwise. Same semantics as OBJECT REFCOUNT now apply consistently across both commands. New regression test debug_object_reports_int_max_refcount_for_shared_integer_value pins both the shared-int case (SET k 42 → refcount:INT_MAX, encoding:int) and the boundary case (SET k 10001 → refcount:1). Existing 2 DEBUG OBJECT tests still pass since their seeded values (non-integer 'value', non-integer literal) correctly resolve to refcount=1. Frame-type divergence (DEBUG OBJECT emits BulkString but upstream emits SimpleString via addReplyStatusFormat) is filed as a separate follow-up. All fr-command lib tests pass; clippy clean. Twenty-ninth concrete fix this session.

title: Fix Lua string.format missing argument and pattern capture sentinel
description:
## Problem 1: string.format silent nil substitution

fr-command/src/lua_eval.rs line 3850: When string.format has more format specifiers than arguments, args.get(arg_idx) returns None which becomes LuaValue::Nil. Real Lua 5.1 raises \"bad argument #N to 'format' (string expected, got no value)\".

Fix: Check if arg_idx >= args.len() before accessing, and return an error.

## Problem 2: LuaCapture::Substring(start, 0) sentinel ambiguity

fr-command/src/lua_eval.rs line 3527: LuaCapture::Substring(start, 0) is used as a sentinel for \"unclosed capture\" (end not yet determined). But 0 is a legitimate end position for an empty capture at the start of the string (e.g., pattern \"()\" at position 0). This causes incorrect pattern matching behavior for these edge cases.

Fix: Use a dedicated sentinel value. Options:
- Use Option<usize> for the end: Substring(usize, Option<usize>) where None = unclosed
- Use usize::MAX as sentinel (captures can't be that long)
- Add a separate variant: OpenCapture(usize) vs Substring(usize, usize)

Recommendation: Option<usize> is cleanest and most Rust-idiomatic.

## Files to Modify

- crates/fr-command/src/lua_eval.rs: Both fixes in the same file

## Testing

- string.format(\"%s%s\", \"a\") should error, not return \"anil\"
- Pattern \"()\" at start of string should capture position 1 (Lua 1-indexed)
close_reason: string.format errors on missing args instead of silent nil; LuaCapture::Substring uses Option<usize> for end position

title: [MOCK] Lua os.clock should not be constant zero
description: mock-code-finder found crates/fr-command/src/lua_eval.rs returning LuaValue::Number(0.0) for os.clock(). Redis exposes os.clock as a real timing function; a constant zero hides elapsed-time behavior from scripts and current conformance only checks type/non-negativity. Replace the constant with a monotonic per-script clock and add focused regression coverage.
close_reason: Completed: os.clock now reports monotonic per-script elapsed time with regression coverage

title: COMMAND INFO movablekeys commands emit empty key_specs (sintercard/zdiff/zunion/etc.)
description:
Several movablekeys commands have empty key_specs in fr but should emit keynum-style keyspec arrays per upstream:

- sintercard, zdiff, zunion, zinter: ven=[RO,access]
- zmpop, blmpop, bzmpop: ven=[RW,access,delete]
- xread, xreadgroup: ven=[RO,access]

These all use keynum find_keys (numkeys at a specific argv position, then keystep=1 keys). The pattern matches the existing eval/fcall override (frankenredis-89b8r) but with a different begin_search index per command.

Subcommand-container commands (xinfo, xgroup, object) emit per-subcommand key_spec arrays in upstream:
- xinfo: ven=[RO,access,RO,access,RO,access] (one per subcommand: STREAM, CONSUMERS, GROUPS)
- xgroup: ven=[RW,delete,RW,delete,RW,update,RW,insert,RW,insert] (per subcommand)
- object: ven=[RO,RO,RO,RO] (one per subcommand: ENCODING, FREQ, IDLETIME, REFCOUNT)

These need a fan-out emission that walks the subcommand list.
close_reason: Added command_info_movable_keynum_keyspec for 11 movablekeys commands (sintercard/zdiff/zunion/zinter/zintercard/zmpop/lmpop/blmpop/bzmpop/xread/xreadgroup). New helper make_keyspec_keyword_range handles XREAD/XREADGROUP keyword begin_search. Pin test covers all 11. Byte-level COMMAND INFO output between fr and ven matches. Subcommand-container fan-out (xinfo/xgroup/object) and sort/sort_ro unknown begin_search tracked in o3xwt. Commit 4160258.

title: Wire SLOWLOG GET/LEN/RESET to real slowlog data in runtime
close_reason: Already implemented: SLOWLOG GET/LEN/RESET are fully wired in fr-runtime::handle_slowlog_command with real slowlog data from ServerState. The fr-command stub is dead code.

title: [parity-gap] Stream max-deleted-id is lost across RENAME/COPY
description:
Review of recent XINFO stream metadata commits found that fr-store keeps max-deleted-entry-id in a side map, but RENAME/RENAMENX/COPY and related key-move helpers only move stream groups, stream_last_ids, and entries_added. The max-deleted-entry-id metadata is left behind or stale at the destination, so XINFO STREAM and lag invalidation can diverge from Redis after moving a stream with deleted entries.

Repro against vendored Redis 7.2.4:
1. Start legacy Redis: legacy_redis_code/redis/src/redis-server --port 63991 --save "" --appendonly no
2. Run:
   XADD s 1-0 f v
   XDEL s 1-0
   XINFO STREAM s
   RENAME s t
   XINFO STREAM t
3. Upstream reports max-deleted-entry-id 1-0 both before and after RENAME. FrankenRedis currently removes/renames groups, last-id, and entries-added metadata, but does not move stream_max_deleted_ids, so the renamed stream reports 0-0.

Acceptance: move/copy/clear stream_max_deleted_ids alongside other stream side metadata for RENAME, RENAMENX, COPY, flush/key removal, prefix/database swaps, and type-changing overwrites; add core_stream conformance coverage for RENAME preserving max-deleted-entry-id.
close_reason: Moved stream max-deleted-entry-id metadata through stream key move/copy/clear paths and added RENAME conformance coverage

title: Lua table.sort: missing comparator arg validation + mixed-type compare not propagating
description:
fr's table.sort has several gaps vs vendored Redis 7.2.4:

1. Non-function comparator: vendored validates the type and emits
   "bad argument #2 to 'sort' (function expected, got TYPE)". fr
   tries to call it and produces the generic "attempt to call a
   STRING/TYPE value" error from the call dispatch path.

   EVAL "local t={1,2,3}; table.sort(t, 'bad'); return t" 0
     vendored: bad argument #2 to 'sort' (function expected, got string)
     fr:       attempt to call a string value

2. Mixed-type sort: vendored propagates the underlying < comparison
   error ("attempt to compare table with string"); fr's default sort
   uses `partial_cmp(...).unwrap_or(Equal)` which silently treats
   incomparable values as equal.

   EVAL "local t={'a',{b=2}}; local ok,e=pcall(table.sort, t); return tostring(e)" 0
     vendored: attempt to compare table with string
     fr:       nil

3. Nil values in array trigger compare-nil error in vendored. fr
   silently sorts whatever's present.

   EVAL "local t={1,nil,3}; table.sort(t); return #t" 0
     vendored: ERR attempt to compare nil with number
     fr:       1

Deferred from this bead (but worth noting): vendored's quicksort
detects when a user comparator violates strict weak ordering ("invalid
order function for sorting") by comparing the same pair both ways at
pivot time. fr uses a naive insertion sort that doesn't make that
check. Implementing it would require a proper Hoare-partition style
sort. File as separate follow-up.

Fix locus: crates/fr-command/src/lua_eval.rs — the 'table.sort' arm.

- Reject non-function comparators with the standard luaL_argerror
  wording via format_builtin_argerror.
- Replace the `unwrap_or(Ordering::Equal)` default with a real Err
  raised when operands are incompatible (LuaValue::Nil, mixed Str
  vs Number, table vs anything, etc.). Propagate via the Result.
- Sort needs to return Result; current sort_by interface doesn't
  support fallible compare. Switch to a manual sort that surfaces
  the error.

Test plan: pin the 3 cases above, plus regressions that sorting
numbers/strings still works.
close_reason: table.sort in crates/fr-command/src/lua_eval.rs now (1) validates the comparator type — non-callable arg 2 raises 'bad argument #2 to sort (function expected, got TYPE)' via format_builtin_argerror, with current_invocation_name toggling the prefix/'?' name; (2) the default insertion sort now propagates the underlying compare error instead of treating incompatible pairs as Equal — mixed Number/Str/Table/Nil raises 'attempt to compare X with Y' with the bare C-level wording (no user_script:1 prefix, matching vendored's C-origin error). Custom-comparator path unchanged. Regression test lua_table_sort_comparator_and_default_compare_b2cmq pins 8 cases including pcall-indirect variant. Live diff vs vendored Redis 7.2.4 on :16380 clean across 8 probes. The 'invalid order function for sorting' detection (strict-weak-ordering violation in user comparators) is a separate piece of work that needs a Hoare-partition-style sort; deferred.

title: [HIGH] PFMERGE TTL verify off-by-2ms (live oracle harness clock drift)
description: Surfaced by live_redis_core_hyperloglog_matches_runtime: single divergence pfmerge_ttl_verify_ttl_after expects Integer(59998) vs our Integer(60000). Upstream's TTL has decremented by the ~2ms of wall-clock elapsed between PFMERGE and the verification PTTL; our fixture clock doesn't advance. Likely a fixture-clock-vs-wall-clock issue, not a functional bug. Fix: either (a) loosen the expected PTTL to a range [59990, 60000] in the fixture, or (b) add a PTTL tolerance in the differential comparator for time-advance cases.
close_reason: Already resolved: core_hyperloglog passes 100% STRICT (PFMERGE TTL canonicalizer in place).

title: EVAL parse error wrong wording — fr emits runtime-shape '<msg> script: <sha>, on @user_script:1.' instead of upstream 'ERR Error compiling script (new function): user_script:1: <msg>' for many compile failures
description:
Differential probe vs vendored 7.2.4: EVAL 'blah blah' 0 emits ERR compile-shape on vendored, ERR runtime-shape on fr.

vendored: ERR Error compiling script (new function): user_script:1: '=' expected near 'blah'
fr     : ERR '=' expected near 'blah' script: 135a1e01bae742c4a576b20fd41a683f6483ca43, on @user_script:1.

Per script_lua.c::luaCallFunction, parse-time errors take a separate format from runtime ones. fr's eval_script_error_reply only routes 'unexpected symbol near', 'unexpected character', 'syntax error' into the compile-error branch. Other parse errors emitted by lua_eval Parser ('X expected near', 'malformed number near', 'unfinished', etc.) fall through and get the runtime suffix.

Cleanest fix: in eval_cmd, call lua_eval::compile_check(script) before eval_script. On Err, route directly to the compile-error reply shape (already used by SCRIPT LOAD per frankenredis-scrldch). Acceptance: EVAL 'blah blah' 0 byte-equal vendored.
close_reason: Pre-validate EVAL bodies via lua_eval::compile_check; route Err to upstream 'ERR Error compiling script (new function): user_script:1: <msg>' shape regardless of which Parser branch produced the wording. Verified byte-equal vs vendored 7.2.4 for blah-blah and @@@ cases; runtime errors still get the SHA1 envelope.

title: Tighten CLIENT REPLY/help/no-evict/no-touch argument validation
description: CLIENT REPLY still accepts arbitrary modes in the live/runtime path and fr-command still treats REPLY/NO-EVICT/NO-TOUCH as unconditional OK. Enforce Redis-style validation and help arity across runtime and command layer.
close_reason: CLIENT validation now matches Redis semantics more closely

title: HIGH: MULTI/EXEC/WATCH linearizability harness — atomicity + abort invariants under interleaved writers
description:
## Summary

MULTI/EXEC/WATCH are present in `fr-runtime/src/lib.rs:1273-1438`, `watched_keys: Vec<(Vec<u8>, u64, u64)>` at line 1686, and there is an `ERR EXEC without MULTI` path at line 9896. But the existing test coverage is command-by-command; nothing validates the two contracts that make transactions useful:

1. **Atomicity:** a full EXEC batch either all-applies or none-applies, with no interleaving observable from any other client.
2. **WATCH abort:** if any watched key is modified by any other client between WATCH and EXEC, EXEC returns nil and applies nothing.

Both contracts are trivially true under the current single-thread eventloop *because* the eventloop serializes command dispatch — but that is an implementation detail, not a guarantee. Any future concurrency refactor (per-shard threads, IO thread split, background AOF apply) can silently break these without a dedicated harness.

## Applying /testing-conformance-harnesses + /testing-metamorphic

This is Pattern 4 (spec-derived test matrix) for the documented contract, plus metamorphic relations that hold under ANY correct implementation regardless of threading model.

### Spec-derived assertions (upstream behavioural contract)

A matrix of scenarios, each running two client sessions A and B against the same Runtime:

| # | A | B interleave | Expected EXEC reply on A |
|---|---|---|---|
| M1 | `MULTI; SET k 1; EXEC`                         | -                             | `["OK"]` |
| M2 | `WATCH k; MULTI; SET k 1; EXEC`                | nothing                       | `["OK"]` |
| M3 | `WATCH k; MULTI; SET k 1; EXEC`                | `SET k 2` between WATCH+EXEC  | `(nil)`  |
| M4 | `WATCH k1 k2; MULTI; SET k1 1; SET k2 2; EXEC` | `DEL k2` between WATCH+EXEC   | `(nil)`  |
| M5 | `WATCH k; MULTI; ...; EXEC; MULTI; SET k 9; EXEC` | -                          | second EXEC unaffected by first abort |
| M6 | `MULTI; INVALIDCMD; SET k 1; EXEC`             | -                             | queue error, EXEC returns `EXECABORT`, key unchanged |
| M7 | `MULTI; DEBUG SLEEP 0.1; EXEC`                 | `GET k` from B mid-EXEC       | B sees the POST-EXEC value |

Matrix runs against both `fr-runtime` and upstream (differential), with all expected replies captured from the live oracle per `/testing-conformance-harnesses` Pattern 1.

### Metamorphic relations

Under any correct transaction implementation:

- **MR-LINEAR:** For ANY interleaved sequence of read-only B-operations around an EXEC batch, every B-read either returns entirely-pre-EXEC state or entirely-post-EXEC state. Never a mix. Encodable as a post-run invariant check over a test log of `(t_observed, k, v)` tuples.
- **MR-WATCH-MONOTONIC:** For watched key `k`, if B executes N writes to k between WATCH and EXEC, A's EXEC must return nil. The count doesn't matter; the flag is binary.
- **MR-WATCH-IDEMPOTENT:** `WATCH k; WATCH k; EXEC; SET k 1` and `WATCH k; EXEC; SET k 1` produce identical observables.
- **MR-UNWATCH-CLEARS:** `WATCH k; <B modifies k>; UNWATCH; WATCH k; MULTI; SET k 1; EXEC` must return `["OK"]` — UNWATCH drops the version-pin so the second WATCH re-captures fresh.
- **MR-DISCONNECT:** A client disconnect between MULTI and EXEC is equivalent to DISCARD — no partial write.

## Harness shape

`crates/fr-conformance/tests/multi_exec_invariants.rs`:
- Table-driven M1..M7 matrix (one test case per row), `#[test]` each.
- Property test using `proptest` over random interleavings: generate a 2-client interleaving script, run it through `Runtime::execute_frame` sessionA/sessionB alternation, check MR-LINEAR via the observation log.
- Differential verification reuses the live-oracle spawner from `frankenredis-5pnv`.

## Non-goals

- Does not attempt multi-key cross-shard transactions (CLUSTER).
- Does not test script-inside-transaction (EVAL inside MULTI) — that is its own beast (script ends MULTI implicitly upstream, per server.c).

## Acceptance

- All seven M* cases pass in both FrankenRedis and upstream (differential).
- Property test runs 10k interleavings without a violation.
- Harness survives a deliberate regression injection (e.g., temporarily comment out the version-bump in the key-write path) by surfacing a clear failing property.

## Labels

fr-runtime, transactions, multi-exec, watch, concurrency, testing-metamorphic, linearizability
close_reason: Fixed in 431fa9d — 11 tests (M1-M5 + 4 MRs + 2 regression guards) pin transaction contract on single-thread eventloop.

title: fr-command: GEODIST with extra trailing args emits wrong-arity instead of upstream syntax error
INFO emits extra trailing CRLF after last section vs vendored 7.2.4
description:
Differential probe vs vendored Redis 7.2.4 (16380):

  INFO server | tail -2 (cat -A):
    fr  : 'listener0:name=tcp,bind=*,bind=-::*,port=16480\r\n\r\n'  (extra blank)
    ven : 'listener0:name=tcp,bind=*,bind=-::*,port=16380\r\n'      (no trailing blank)

Same +1 line discrepancy across every INFO section (server, clients, memory, persistence, stats, replication, cpu, commandstats, latencystats, keyspace, cluster).

Cause: fr-command/src/lib.rs's info_command_str appends 'info.push_str("\r\n");' (the section separator blank) after every section's content, including the last. Upstream emits the separator BETWEEN sections only — never after the last.

Fix options:
  (1) In fr-runtime/src/lib.rs::handle_info_command, trim a trailing '\r\n' from the final concatenated info bytes before returning the BulkString.
  (2) In fr-command/src/lib.rs::info_command_str, refactor to track the last-emitted section and skip its trailing separator.

Option (1) is the smaller surgical fix and was applied here.
close_reason:
handle_info_command in fr-runtime now trims a single trailing '\\r\\n' from the concatenated info bytes before returning the BulkString — matches upstream INFO which emits the section separator BETWEEN sections only, never after the last. Per-section requests now emit the same byte count as vendored 7.2.4.

Verified vs vendored 7.2.4 (16380):
  All sections that previously had +1 line (server/clients/memory/persistence/stats/replication/cpu/keyspace/cluster) now match exactly.
  commandstats/latencystats/errorstats remaining differences are session-specific (vendored has long uptime + diverse command history; fr just started), not wire-shape defects.

Tagalong fix: realigned 4 stale fixtures in core_server.json (info_cluster_section, info_modules_section, info_keyspace_section_empty_db, info_keyspace_section_with_key) to drop the trailing '\\r\\n'. Updated 1 in-tree pin (info_errorstats_emits_per_error_code_counts) similarly.

Pinned via info_per_section_reply_omits_trailing_inter_section_separator_per_upstream covering server/clients/cpu/errorstats. 889/889 fr-runtime+fr-command lib + 192/193 fr-conformance pass (1 pre-existing live-oracle failure unrelated).

title: rv89 part 3: lazy expiry hook + HGETEX/HGETDEL + HDEL cleanup (fr-command/fr-store)
description:
Third slice of br-frankenredis-rv89. Wire lazy per-field TTL expiry into the existing hash-read surface so expired fields are invisible to HGET/HMGET/HEXISTS/HGETALL/HKEYS/HVALS/HLEN/HSCAN.

- fr-store: on every hash read/write path call drop_hash_field_if_expired for the touched fields.
- fr-command: HGETEX key [EX sec | PX ms | EXAT unix-sec | PXAT unix-ms | PERSIST] FIELDS n field ... (sets/drops TTL in the same read).
- fr-command: HGETDEL key FIELDS n field ... (read value then delete each field).
- HDEL already deletes fields; hook clears field TTL from hash_field_expires too.
- Unit tests: each read path does NOT return expired fields; HGETEX roundtrip; HGETDEL roundtrip; HDEL clears the field's TTL.
close_reason: Landed in b16a58a: lazy per-field TTL expiry wired into every hash-read path (hget/hexists/hmget/hlen/hgetall/hkeys/hvals/hstrlen/hscan/hrandfield*); HDEL clears per-field TTL entries; whole-key removal clears all field TTLs; reaping the last field deletes the hash. 7 new tests (23 total in hash_field_ttl.rs); fr-store 280/0, fr-command 619/0, fr-runtime 272/0. HGETEX/HGETDEL deferred to part 4 (3jrv) alongside encoding / RDB / AOF work.

title: string.format accepts >5 flag chars (vendored 'invalid format (repeated flags)')
description:
Upstream Lua 5.1.5 lstrlib.c::scanformat uses
  (size_t)(p - strfrmt) >= sizeof(FLAGS)
to bound the flag-consumption loop, where FLAGS = "-+ #0" (5 chars
+ null = 6 bytes). Consuming 6 or more flag chars raises
'invalid format (repeated flags)'. fr's format parser does not
track flag-char count and silently accepts any number of repeated
or duplicated flag chars.

Repro:
  EVAL "local ok,e = pcall(string.format, '%-+ #00d', 1); return tostring(ok)..':'..tostring(e)" 0
    vendored: false:invalid format (repeated flags)
    fr:       true:+1

  Boundary:
    '%-+ #0d'  (5 flag chars) → both OK
    '%-+ #00d' (6 flag chars) → vendored errors, fr accepts

Fix at the flag-parsing loop in fr-command/src/lua_eval.rs near
line 9085: count consumed flag chars, raise the upstream wording
when count > 5.
close_reason: completed

title: [HIGH] CLIENT REPLY OFF wedges the single-client harness (conformance limitation)
description: Surfaced by live_redis_core_client_matches_runtime: CLIENT REPLY OFF tells the server to stop sending replies; the single-TCP-connection harness then waits for a reply that never comes and times out. Fix: either XFAIL client_reply_off cases or teach the live-oracle harness to detect CLIENT REPLY OFF and skip reads for subsequent commands until CLIENT REPLY ON. Separate line: add a harness case-inspector that recognises no-reply commands.

title: CLUSTER NODES still stubbed when cluster_enabled=true; ship synthesized single-self node line
description: Follow-up to frankenredis-39rkv. Upstream cluster.c::clusterGenNodeDescription:5251-5328 emits per-node lines '<id> <ip:port@cport[,hostname]> <flags> <slaveof_or_dash> <pings> <pongs> <epoch> <link> <slot_ranges...>'. fr's CLUSTER NODES still returned cluster_disabled even with cluster_enabled=true. Synthesize one self line: id=server_run_id, ip:port@cport=127.0.0.1:<port>@<port+10000>, flags='myself,master', slaveof='-', pings=0, pongs=0, epoch=0, link='connected', slots=merged ranges from cluster_assigned_slots.
close_reason: fixed

title: string.format panics fr-server on no-args, plus 4 wording/validation divergences
description:
Multiple bugs in fr's string.format (crates/fr-command/src/lua_eval.rs:5196 and lua_string_format at :6813):

1. **DENIAL OF SERVICE (P0):** Any authenticated client can crash fr-server with
     EVAL 'return string.format()' 0
   fr panics with 'range start index 1 out of range for slice of length 0' at lua_eval.rs:5202 because the cjson.encode arm slices args[1..] without checking that args is non-empty. The panic kills the entire server process. Vendored emits:
     ERR user_script:1: bad argument #1 to 'format' (string expected, got no value) script: <sha>, on @user_script:1.

2. **No-value error off-by-one (line 6877):** fr emits 'bad argument #1 to format (no value)' for a missing value; vendored emits 'bad argument #2 to format (no value)'. The format string itself is Lua arg #1; the first format value is arg #2. fr's helper uses arg_idx + 1 where arg_idx is 0 (before increment), giving 1 instead of 2. The '%s' branch at line 6952 already gets this right (uses arg_idx after the +=1 plus another +1) — the no-value path needs the same +2.

3. **Invalid spec silently ignored (line 7016):** string.format('%K', 5) on vendored emits 'invalid option "%K" to format'. fr's catch-all '_ => format!("%{conv}")' silently emits literal '%K'.

4. **Non-number arg to numeric specs silently converts to 0 (lines 6886, 6897, 6909, 6920, 6989, 7004, 7013):** string.format('%d', 'abc') / true / nil / {} all return '0' on fr; vendored errors 'bad argument #2 to format (number expected, got <type>)'. fr uses arg.to_number().unwrap_or(0.0) — should error on None.

5. **%c wrap-around semantics (line 7013):** fr does 'n as u8' which Rust saturates: -1 → 0, 256 → 255. Vendored uses C-style (char)n which wraps modulo 256: -1 → 255 (0xFF), 256 → 0 (NUL). Lower priority — only affects byte values.

Out of scope:
- %s with __tostring metatable already works in fr (verified live)
- Existing %s non-string error already matches vendored
- 'string expected, got no value' for arg #1 (the format string) when called as string.format() with no args — needs the format string itself to be validated before slicing.

Fix sketch:
  - cjson.encode arm: check args.is_empty() → return Err('user_script:1: bad argument #1 to format (string expected, got no value)') before the args[1..] slice.
  - lua_string_format no-value branch: arg_idx + 2, with user_script:1: prefix.
  - Catch-all conversion branch: Err('user_script:1: invalid option "%{conv}" to format').
  - %d/%i/%u/%x/%X/%o/%c/%f/%e/%E/%g/%G: gate arg.to_number() with explicit None → Err('user_script:1: bad argument #N to format (number expected, got {type})').
  - %c: use ((n as i64) as u8) for vendored wrap-around.

Test plan:
  - DOS regression: string.format() with no args returns the standard error string and does NOT kill the process; pin via dispatch_argv test.
  - %d with: 42, '42', ' 42 ', 0x10, 1.7, -1.7, true, nil, {}, 'abc' — only the last 4 must error, with the right type name.
  - %K (invalid) → error.
  - %s with bool/nil/table — unchanged (still error correctly).
  - %c with: 65, -1, 256, 65.7 — must match vendored byte output.
close_reason:
Fixed P0 DoS: string.format() with no args no longer panics fr-server. Plus 3 wording fixes that match vendored verbatim:

1. (DOS) string.format() with no args: previously panicked at args[1..] slice and killed the process. Now validates the format-string arg explicitly with luaL_checkstring wording: 'user_script:1: bad argument #1 to format (string expected, got no value)' (or 'got <type>' for non-string non-number arg #1). Caller code uses a safe args[1..] slice guarded by args.is_empty().

2. No-value error off-by-one (line 6877 → now 6883): emits 'bad argument #2' for missing %d arg, not '#1'. arg_idx + 2 accounts for the format string itself being Lua arg #1. Added user_script:1: prefix to match upstream's auto-prepended source location.

3. Invalid spec '%K' silently ignored: now errors 'user_script:1: invalid option "%K" to format' matching upstream lstrlib.c:str_format.

4. Non-number args to numeric specs (%d/%i/%u/%f/%e/%E/%g/%G/%x/%X/%o/%c): previously coerced via .to_number().unwrap_or(0.0); now uses a require_number() helper that returns 'user_script:1: bad argument #N to format (number expected, got <type>)'. Validated for string/boolean/nil/table inputs.

5. %c integer wrap (line 7013): now uses 'as i64 as u8' so 256 → 0 (modulo-256 wrap) rather than Rust's saturating cast (256 → 255). The ASCII path is regression-tested. The high-byte UTF-8 encoding gap (-1 → 0xFF emits 2 bytes [0xC3, 0xBF] in fr's String-typed result vs vendored's single 0xFF byte) and vendored's empty-output quirk for %c 256 both require lua_string_format to return Vec<u8> instead of String — out of scope for this fix.

Live diff vs vendored 7.2.4 (port 16380): 6 error cases and 11 happy-path cases match byte-for-byte. fr-server confirmed alive after the DOS regression case.

Pinned by:
- string_format_no_args_does_not_panic_and_matches_upstream
- string_format_validates_specs_and_args_match_upstream (6 error cases + 7 happy paths)
- string_format_c_specifier_ascii_path

All 936 fr-command lib tests pass (+3 new).

title: EPIC: Close remaining throughput gap to Redis (post-recovery Phase 2)
description:
# EPIC: Throughput Optimization Campaign — Phase 2

## Context & History

On April 7, 2026, the first FrankenRedis live-server benchmarks revealed a
catastrophic throughput gap: ~1200 ops/sec vs Redis's ~95,000 ops/sec on per-
command workloads, and ~1200 ops/sec vs Redis's ~860,000 on pipeline=16. That's
1.3% of Redis throughput — 75x slower.

On April 9, 2026 (bead frankenredis-zjii, now closed), profile-driven root-cause
analysis identified two smoking guns:

1. **Store::state_digest() called on every command** (80% of CPU): An O(N_keys)
   full-database hash computed eagerly for the threat-event forensics ledger,
   which only fires on rare error paths. The success path (99.99% of commands)
   discarded the result.

2. **ACL command_matches_acl_category() on every command** (71% of CPU after fix
   #1): The is_command_allowed check computed command_categories() — iterating
   21 ACL categories × 200 commands × split_whitespace().collect::<Vec<_>>() =
   ~4,200 Vec allocations per command — even when the user had no category-level
   ACL rules at all.

Both were fixed. FrankenRedis now runs at **79–99% of Redis throughput on per-
command workloads** and **31% on pipeline=16**:

| Workload | Pipeline | Before | After | vs Redis 7.2.4 |
|----------|----------|--------|-------|----------------|
| SET      | 1        | 1240   | 75054 | 79%            |
| GET      | 1        | 1204   | 90567 | 99%            |
| INCR     | 1        | 1176   | 81383 | 86%            |
| MIXED    | 1        | 1159   | 80372 | 83%            |
| SET      | 16       | 1221   | 268414| 31%            |

## What This Epic Covers

This epic tracks the **remaining optimization work** to close the gap further,
especially on pipelined workloads. The Phase 1 recovery is done; this is Phase 2.

The final flamegraph (April 9) after both Phase 1 fixes shows a healthy profile:

```
12.73%  __memcmp_avx2_movbe        (HashMap/BTreeMap key comparisons)
 5.70%  cfree                      (allocator)
 4.45%  Store::drop_if_expired     (per-command lazy expiry)
 4.36%  Store::exists              (key lookup)
 3.76%  Store::record_ops_sec_sample
 3.13%  malloc                     (allocator)
 2.93%  BTreeMap::insert           (store insert — O(log n) instead of O(1))
```

There is no single smoking gun left; instead, several smaller optimizations
each contribute a few percentage points. The biggest structural opportunity is
replacing BTreeMap with HashMap for Store::entries (P1).

## Proof Artifacts

- artifacts/optimization/throughput-gap/before/SUMMARY.md — Phase 1 baseline
- artifacts/optimization/throughput-gap/before/perf_report.txt — 80% state_digest
- artifacts/optimization/throughput-gap/after/perf_report_final.txt — healthy profile
- artifacts/optimization/throughput-gap/ISOMORPHISM_PROOF_LAZY_DIGEST.md — full proof

## How This Serves the Project

The README states: "Deterministic Latency Replication Core (DLRC): strict command
semantics with tail-aware scheduling and recoverable persistence pipelines." This
is the #1 differentiator claim. If FrankenRedis doesn't perform within striking
distance of Redis, the project has no credible pitch. The Phase 1 recovery moved
us from "embarrassingly slow" to "competitive"; Phase 2 aims for "impressive."

## Methodology Constraint

All optimization work MUST follow the extreme-software-optimization loop:
1. Baseline (record p50/p95/p99 and memory)
2. Profile (identify real hotspots with flamegraph evidence)
3. Implement ONE optimization lever per commit
4. Prove behavior unchanged via conformance + invariant checks
5. Re-baseline and emit delta artifact
close_reason: All Phase 2 optimization subtasks completed. Final benchmark: SET p1=69.6K (74% Redis), GET p1=75.5K (83% Redis), SET p16=284K (33% Redis), GET p16=450K (47% Redis). Major improvements from: HashMap migration, write coalescing, double-parse elimination, allocator evaluation.

3. frankenredis-be8t.1 (double frame_to_argv) — easy win ~2-3%

title: Eliminate double frame_to_argv parse in execute_frame_internal
close_reason: Implemented: frame_to_argv now called once in execute_frame(), result passed to execute_frame_internal(). All 43 conformance tests pass. Benchmark shows modest improvement (SET p1: ~78K→~80K, SET p16: ~275K stable). No regression.

title: Implement per-poll-cycle write coalescing in fr-server event loop
description:
# Per-Poll-Cycle Write Coalescing

## What

The fr-server event loop (crates/fr-server/src/main.rs) currently processes
commands one at a time and writes responses immediately after each command.
For pipelined workloads (pipeline=16), this means 16 separate write() syscalls
per client per batch, instead of coalescing all 16 responses into a single
write.

## Why Pipeline=16 Is Only 31% of Redis

Redis coalesces pipelined responses into a single output buffer per client
and flushes once per poll cycle. This amortizes:
- write() syscall overhead (~1us per syscall)
- TCP Nagle/cork interactions
- Kernel buffer management

At 50 clients × 16 pipeline depth, FrankenRedis makes 800 write() calls
per poll cycle vs Redis's ~50. That's a 16x syscall amplification that
directly limits pipeline throughput.

## Design

### Option A: Per-client output buffer
Accumulate response bytes into a per-client Vec<u8> buffer. After processing
all readable frames for a client in a poll cycle, flush the buffer in a
single write().

### Option B: writev() scatter-gather
Collect response byte slices as an iovec array and use writev() to send
them in a single syscall without copying into a contiguous buffer.

### Recommended: Option A first
Simpler, safer, works with mio. Add a write_buf: Vec<u8> to each client
connection. After processing all frames from a readable event, if write_buf
is non-empty, register interest in WRITABLE and flush on the next writable
event. This is the standard mio pattern.

## Verification

- strace -c the server under pipeline=16 load before and after
- Count write() syscalls per second — should drop ~16x
- Benchmark pipeline=16 SET — target >500K ops/sec (from current 268K)

## Acceptance Criteria

1. Pipelined responses coalesced into single write per client per poll cycle
2. strace confirms write() count reduced proportional to pipeline depth
3. pipeline=16 throughput improves significantly (>30% improvement)
4. pipeline=1 throughput not regressed
5. All conformance tests pass
close_reason: Already implemented: write coalescing exists at line 940. Pipeline scaling verified: p1=78K, p4=172K (2.2x), p16=283K (3.6x). Another agent added additional improvements deferring flush to writable handler.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text:
[TopazCreek 2026-04-09] Pipeline=16 investigation notes:

Current pipeline=16 SET throughput: 268K ops/sec vs Redis's 860K = 31%. The gap is NOT per-command overhead (per-command is 79-99% of Redis). It's amortization efficiency — Redis batches responses in write coalescing.

Verification: strace -c under pipeline=16 load — count write() syscalls. If write count ≈ ops_count instead of ops_count/pipeline_depth, that confirms response-per-write.

mio pattern: after processing all frames from a READABLE event, if there's pending output, reregister token with READABLE|WRITABLE. On WRITABLE event flush buffer and switch back.

title: Reduce RespFrame::to_bytes heap allocations via pre-sized buffers
description:
# Reduce RespFrame::to_bytes Heap Allocations

## What

RespFrame::to_bytes() (crates/fr-protocol/src/lib.rs) creates a new Vec<u8>
for every response frame. For simple responses like +OK or :1, this allocates
a tiny heap buffer, which is immediately written to the socket and freed.

In the April 9 flamegraph, cfree (5.70%) and malloc (3.13%) together consume
~9% of CPU. A significant portion comes from per-response Vec<u8> allocations
in to_bytes and similar serialization paths.

## Design Options

### Option A: encode_into(&mut Vec<u8>)
Already partially implemented (commit 2813053: "perf(fr-protocol): avoid
intermediate String allocations in RespFrame::encode_into"). Extend this
pattern to all response-serialization paths. Pass a reusable buffer that grows
but never shrinks, amortizing allocation across commands.

### Option B: SmallVec or stack-allocated buffers
For simple responses (OK, integers, short errors), use a stack buffer
(e.g., [u8; 64]) and only heap-allocate for large responses. This avoids
malloc entirely for the common case.

### Option C: Write directly to socket buffer
Instead of materializing the response as bytes then copying to socket,
write RESP framing directly into the client's output buffer. This eliminates
the intermediate allocation entirely. Best combined with write coalescing.

### Recommended: Start with Option A (extend encode_into)
It already exists; just wire it through all response paths. Benchmark, then
consider Option C as a follow-up if allocation is still dominant.

## Note on Recent Optimization

Commit 2813053 already optimized encode_into to avoid intermediate String
allocations. This bead extends that work to the remaining per-response
allocation paths.

## Acceptance Criteria

1. No new Vec<u8> allocation per simple response (+OK, :N, -ERR)
2. malloc/cfree percentage in flamegraph drops measurably
3. All conformance tests pass
4. Benchmark shows improvement (target: >5% on pipeline workloads)
close_reason: Implemented: changed 12 hot-path .to_bytes() calls to .encode_into(&mut write_buf), eliminating intermediate Vec allocations. All 43 conformance tests pass. Performance stable (SET p1: ~80K, SET p16: ~275K). Code is now allocation-free for response serialization.

title: Amortize active expire cycle cost across tick budget
description:
# Amortize Active Expire Cycle Across Tick Budget

## What

execute_frame_internal (crates/fr-runtime/src/lib.rs line ~2979) calls
self.run_active_expire_cycle(now_ms, ActiveExpireCycleKind::Fast) before
EVERY general command dispatch. Additionally, the server event loop calls
it once per tick at line ~487.

The per-command call means that for a pipeline of 16 commands, the expire
cycle runs 16 times (plus once in the tick). Redis runs the expire cycle
once per event loop iteration (beforeSleep), not per command.

## Why This Matters

Store::drop_if_expired was 4.45% of CPU in the April 9 flamegraph, and
run_active_expire_cycle adds additional sampling overhead. With 50 clients
at pipeline=16, that's 800+ expire cycle invocations per tick vs Redis's 1.

## Design

Remove the per-command active-expire call from execute_frame_internal.
The server event loop's per-tick call (line ~487) is sufficient — this
matches Redis's beforeSleep pattern.

Individual commands that need lazy expiry (GET, EXISTS, etc.) already
call drop_if_expired on their specific key. The active expire cycle is
for proactive background cleanup, not per-command correctness.

## Risk

If the tick rate is too low (10ms default poll timeout when idle), expired
keys may linger slightly longer. This is acceptable — Redis has the same
behavior. The lazy expiry on access ensures no expired key is ever returned
to a client.

## Acceptance Criteria

1. run_active_expire_cycle called only in server tick, not per-command
2. Expired keys still never returned to clients (lazy expiry on access)
3. All conformance tests pass (especially expiry-related ones)
4. Benchmark shows measurable improvement on high-pipeline workloads
close_reason: Completed: active expire cycle only in server tick

title: Evaluate jemalloc/mimalloc as global allocator for fr-server
description:
# Evaluate Alternative Global Allocators

## What

The April 9 flamegraph shows malloc (3.13%) + cfree (5.70%) + _int_malloc
(2.42%) = ~11% of CPU spent in the glibc allocator. Redis uses jemalloc by
default, which is optimized for the many-small-allocation pattern of
key-value stores.

## Why

glibc malloc is a general-purpose allocator. For workloads with many small,
short-lived allocations (RESP frame parsing, response serialization, key
copies), specialized allocators can provide:
- Lower fragmentation
- Better cache locality (thread-local arenas)
- Faster small-allocation paths (slab allocators)
- Reduced lock contention (though FrankenRedis is single-threaded, allocator
  internal locks still exist in glibc)

## Candidates

### jemalloc (via tikv-jemallocator crate)
- Redis's default allocator
- Excellent for mixed-size workloads
- Well-tested in production Redis deployments
- ~200KB binary size increase

### mimalloc (via mimalloc crate)
- Microsoft's allocator, optimized for modern workloads
- Often faster than jemalloc for small allocations
- Lower memory overhead
- ~100KB binary size increase

## Implementation

Add to crates/fr-server/Cargo.toml:
```toml
[dependencies]
tikv-jemallocator = { version = "0.6", optional = true }
```

In main.rs:
```rust
#[cfg(feature = "jemalloc")]
#[global_allocator]
static GLOBAL: tikv_jemallocator::Jemalloc = tikv_jemallocator::Jemalloc;
```

## Verification

Benchmark with and without the feature flag:
1. fr-bench all workloads (set/get/incr/mixed, p1 and p16)
2. Flamegraph comparison — allocator functions should drop from ~11% to <5%
3. Memory usage comparison under sustained load (100K keys, 1M operations)
4. No unsafe code introduction — the allocator crate handles this

## Note on #![forbid(unsafe_code)]

The global allocator crates use unsafe internally but present a safe API.
The #[global_allocator] attribute is safe Rust. The fr-server binary can
use this without violating the unsafe_code forbid on the library crates.

## Acceptance Criteria

1. Benchmark comparison (jemalloc vs glibc) for all workloads
2. If improvement >5%, add jemalloc as default feature for fr-server
3. If improvement <5%, close as "not worth the dependency"
4. Memory usage not significantly increased (< 10% overhead)
5. All tests pass with the new allocator
close_reason: Completed evaluation. Allocators are wired up correctly.

title: Investigate redundant drop_if_expired/exists calls per command path
description:
# Investigate Redundant Expiry Checks Per Command

## What

In the April 9 flamegraph, Store::drop_if_expired is 4.45% and Store::exists
is 4.36% of CPU. These functions perform key lookups with expiry checks. The
question is whether multiple expiry checks fire for the same key within a
single command path.

## Hypothesis

A typical SET command path may traverse:
1. execute_frame_internal → dispatch
2. dispatch_argv → set handler
3. set handler calls self.entries.get/get_mut (which might call drop_if_expired)
4. set handler calls exists/drop_if_expired explicitly before insert
5. If SETEX/PSETEX: additional expire path

If drop_if_expired is called 2-3 times for the same key in the same command,
the second and third calls are wasted — the key can't re-expire between calls
within a synchronous single-threaded execution.

## Investigation Steps

1. Trace all call sites of drop_if_expired in fr-store/src/lib.rs
2. For each Store method (set_string, get_string, exists, etc.), count how
   many times drop_if_expired is called per logical operation
3. Identify redundant calls where the same key is checked multiple times
4. Propose consolidation: either check once at entry point, or use a
   "just checked" sentinel

## Design for Fix (if redundancy confirmed)

### Option A: Check-once wrapper
Each Store method that touches a key calls drop_if_expired once at the top
and marks the entry as "checked this tick." Subsequent calls within the same
now_ms timestamp skip the check.

### Option B: Caller-side dedup
Move the drop_if_expired call to the dispatch layer (execute_db_scoped_command)
and remove it from individual Store methods. The dispatcher pre-expires all
keys the command will touch, then the Store methods can assume keys are live.

### Option C: Lazy-only
Don't proactively expire at all in the per-command path. Only expire when a
key is accessed and found to be expired. This is Redis's actual behavior
(lazy expiry + periodic background expiry). The per-access check is just
"if expired, delete and return nil."

Recommended: Option C if conformance allows — it matches Redis most closely.

## Acceptance Criteria

1. Investigation report identifying all redundant expiry check paths
2. If redundancy confirmed: fix reduces drop_if_expired CPU share
3. All expiry conformance tests still pass
4. TTL/PTTL commands still return correct values
close_reason: Reduced redundant drop_if_expired lookups in SET/EXPIRETIME/XADD/LPOP/RPOP paths

title: Phase 2 comprehensive re-baseline: flamegraph + benchmark + delta artifact after all optimizations
description:
# Phase 2 Comprehensive Re-Baseline

## What

After all Phase 2 optimization subtasks are complete (or a meaningful subset),
run a comprehensive benchmark suite + flamegraph capture and produce a final
delta artifact comparing Phase 2 results to both:
- The original April 7 baseline (frankenredis-zjii era: ~1200 ops/sec)
- The April 9 Phase 1 recovery baseline (~75K-90K ops/sec per-command)
- The Redis 7.2.4 reference numbers

## Steps

1. Clean build: cargo build --release -p fr-server -p fr-bench
2. Start server: ./target/release/frankenredis --port 6399 --mode strict
3. Run fr-bench for ALL workloads at pipeline=1 and pipeline=16:
   set, get, incr, lpush, lpop, hset, hget, mixed
4. Capture perf flamegraph (-e cpu-clock -F 999) under pipeline=16 load
5. Capture strace -c under pipeline=16 load (verify syscall reduction)
6. Compare numbers to all previous baselines
7. Write artifacts/optimization/phase2-final/DELTA_REPORT.md with:
   - Before/after table for all workloads
   - Flamegraph comparison (what changed, what's left)
   - Syscall count comparison
   - Per-optimization-lever attribution (which commit contributed what)
8. Update README.md benchmark section
9. Update baselines/ with new checked-in JSON artifacts

## Target Numbers

Based on the optimization opportunities identified:
- SET p1: >85K ops/sec (from 75K) — HashMap alone should get this
- GET p1: >95K ops/sec (from 90K) — should match or exceed Redis
- SET p16: >400K ops/sec (from 268K) — write coalescing is the lever
- GET p16: >600K ops/sec (from 434K) — same lever

## This Is the Final Gate

This bead should only be claimed after the high-impact subtasks are done.
It produces the evidence that justifies closing the epic.

## Acceptance Criteria

1. Complete benchmark table for all workloads at p1 and p16
2. Flamegraph showing no single function >10% (healthy distribution)
3. Delta report artifact checked in
4. README updated with final numbers
5. baselines/ updated with new JSON artifacts
close_reason: Phase 2 final benchmark completed: SET p1=69.6K (74% Redis), GET p1=75.5K (83% Redis), SET p16=284K, GET p16=450K. Delta report written to artifacts/optimization/phase2-final/DELTA_REPORT.md

title: Freeze Redis config vertical-tab comment handling
description: Add conformance coverage that config lines prefixed by vertical tab before # are not skipped as comments, matching Redis config.c sdstrim(" \t\r\n") before sdssplitargs whitespace handling.
close_reason: Added vertical-tab-prefixed hash config conformance coverage

title: ZPOPMIN/ZPOPMAX with >3 args returns wrong-args, vendored returns syntax error
description:
Vendored Redis 7.2.4's `zpopMinMaxCommand` (t_zset.c:4028) explicitly checks `c->argc > 3` and emits `shared.syntaxerr` ("ERR syntax error"), not the generic arity error. fr's dispatcher rejects too-many-args earlier with the generic 'wrong number of arguments' message.

Reproduction:
  ZADD z 1 a 2 b
  ZPOPMIN z 1 extra
    vendored: ERR syntax error
    fr:       ERR wrong number of arguments for 'zpopmin' command

  ZPOPMIN z 1 extra1 extra2
    vendored: ERR syntax error
    fr:       ERR wrong number of arguments for 'zpopmin' command

Confirmed equally on ZPOPMAX (shared handler).

Other arities match:
  ZPOPMIN z abc  -> both: "ERR value is out of range, must be positive"
  ZPOPMIN z -1   -> both: "ERR value is out of range, must be positive"

Fix: ZPOPMIN/ZPOPMAX should accept argc > 3 at dispatch and return 'ERR syntax error' from the handler when argc > 3 — mirroring upstream's order of checks. Add a regression test pinning both messages.

Probe sweep #115 (ZPOPMIN extra args, inline).
close_reason: completed

title: XGROUP CREATE/SETID arity wording matches upstream commands.def -5 table-level
description: fr-command xgroup CREATE/SETID returned subcommand-syntax-error 'unknown subcommand or wrong number of arguments for X. Try XGROUP HELP.' for argc<5; upstream commands.def declares both with arity -5 so argc<5 emits the table-level 'wrong number of arguments for xgroup|create / xgroup|setid command' wording. The subcommand-syntax-error path remains for argc>=5 with bad option tokens (handler-level addReplySubcommandSyntaxError fall-through)

title: Wire CONFIG SET maxmemory-samples to eviction subsystem
description:
## Problem

fr-runtime/src/lib.rs lines 961-962:
- maxmemory_eviction_sample_limit: 16 (Redis default is 5, called maxmemory-samples)
- maxmemory_eviction_max_cycles: 4

These are initialized in ServerState::default but neither is wired to CONFIG SET maxmemory-samples. Our default (16) is also higher than Redis's default (5), which affects eviction behavior fidelity.

## What Redis Does

CONFIG SET maxmemory-samples <count> changes the number of keys sampled during each eviction cycle. Higher values give better eviction accuracy at the cost of more CPU.

## Implementation Plan

1. Change default from 16 to 5 (match Redis)
2. Handle CONFIG SET maxmemory-samples → update maxmemory_eviction_sample_limit
3. CONFIG GET maxmemory-samples → return current value

## Files to Modify

- crates/fr-runtime/src/lib.rs: Fix default, add CONFIG SET handler
close_reason: maxmemory-samples: default fixed to 5 (was 16), CONFIG SET/GET wired to runtime

title: Restore Beads backlog from documented FrankenRedis parity gaps
description: The repo currently has no active Beads issues even though README.md and FEATURE_PARITY.md explicitly document remaining work. Recreate tracked issues for the highest-priority open gaps and keep Agent Mail/threading aligned to those IDs.
