title: fr-command: XCLAIM error wording divergences (min-idle-time, IDLE/TIME/RETRYCOUNT, Unrecognized option)
description:
Multiple wording divergences in XCLAIM vs vendored redis 7.2.4 t_stream.c::xclaimCommand:

- min-idle-time non-numeric: fr 'value is not an integer or out of range' → upstream 'Invalid min-idle-time argument for XCLAIM' (also: upstream silently clamps negative to 0 instead of erroring).
- IDLE/TIME/RETRYCOUNT non-numeric or negative: fr 'value is not an integer or out of range' → upstream 'Invalid <NAME> option argument for XCLAIM'.
- IDLE/RETRYCOUNT/LASTID without value (last token): fr 'wrong number of arguments' → upstream 'Unrecognized XCLAIM option <NAME>' (the && moreargs guard means the option name itself isn't recognized when no value follows).
- Unknown option token: fr 'Invalid stream ID specified as stream command argument' (because the ID-parser fired first) → upstream 'Unrecognized XCLAIM option <NAME>' (parser breaks on first unparseable ID and continues to options).
- Generic syntax error fallthrough: fr SyntaxError → upstream 'Unrecognized XCLAIM option <NAME>'.

Fix:
- Switch the ID-collection loop to break on the first unparseable token (mirrors upstream's NULL-client streamParseStrictIDOrReply).
- Drop the empty-IDs-vector wrong-arity check; let the option parser surface 'Unrecognized XCLAIM option …' for that case.
- Per-option errors threaded with the upstream wording.
- Silently clamp negative min-idle-time to 0.

title: fr-command LCS DP-table oversize error wording is invented; upstream emits 'Insufficient memory, transient memory for LCS exceeds proto-max-bulk-len'
description:
REVIEW MODE Tick 14 finding via /testing-conformance-harnesses sweep on LCS error wording vs vendored t_string.c::lcsCommand.

UPSTREAM ERRORS (t_string.c lines 749, 792, 812, 818):
  1. 'The specified keys must contain string values' — wrong type
  2. 'String too long for LCS' — sdslen(a) or sdslen(b) >= UINT32_MAX-1
  3. 'Insufficient memory, transient memory for LCS exceeds proto-max-bulk-len' — DP table > proto_max_bulk_len
  4. 'Insufficient memory, failed allocating transient memory for LCS' — ztrymalloc returned NULL

FR CURRENT (crates/fr-command/src/lib.rs lines 11400-11403, 11445-11448):
  'ERR The strings are too long to compute the LCS without LEN flag.'

This wording is invented — it appears nowhere in upstream Redis source. Both compute_lcs (plain LCS reply) and compute_lcs_matches (IDX mode) gate their DP allocations through `m.saturating_mul(n) > LCS_MAX_DP_SIZE` (16M entries) and emit the invented message on overflow. fr also caps 8× lower than upstream (proto_max_bulk_len = 512 MB → 128M u32 entries).

The wording change is the lowest-risk part — bringing fr's reply in line with the upstream-documented error a client would scrape for. The cap-policy change (16M → 128M entries) is a separate decision; deferring.

Severity: [LOW]. Wire-format conformance gap. A client suite that triggers the LCS oversize path would see a string fr invented instead of upstream's documented message.

FIX:
  Replace 'ERR The strings are too long to compute the LCS without LEN flag.' with 'ERR Insufficient memory, transient memory for LCS exceeds proto-max-bulk-len' in both compute_lcs and compute_lcs_matches.
close_reason:
Replaced fr's invented LCS oversize error wording with upstream's documented message at t_string.c:812.

CHANGE (crates/fr-command/src/lib.rs):
Both compute_lcs (plain LCS) and compute_lcs_matches (IDX mode) emitted 'ERR The strings are too long to compute the LCS without LEN flag.' on m*n > LCS_MAX_DP_SIZE — a wording fr invented. Now emit 'ERR Insufficient memory, transient memory for LCS exceeds proto-max-bulk-len', matching upstream addReplyError at t_string.c:812.

Regression test lcs_oversize_emits_upstream_proto_max_bulk_len_wording exercises both paths via dispatch_argv with 4097-byte strings (m*n = 16,785,409 > LCS_MAX_DP_SIZE=16,777,216). The early bail-out fires before DP allocation, so the test stays cheap.

The cap-policy gap (fr 16M entries vs upstream 128M entries from proto_max_bulk_len/sizeof(uint32_t)) is deferred — that's a memory-policy decision, not an error-message bug.

Verification:
- cargo test -p fr-command --lib lcs → 6/6 pass.
- cargo clippy -p fr-command --lib --no-deps -- -D warnings → clean.

[LOW] severity. Wire-format conformance gap.

title: run_fixture: reset shared session replica state per-case instead of swapping sessions; restores fr_p2c_004/scripting/smoke fixtures broken by replconfisolation
description:
b0aa553 (replconfisolation) used with_isolated_session for ALL dedicated-connection commands in run_fixture (HELLO/AUTH/CLIENT/SELECT/RESET/REPLCONF/...) to fix core_replication. That broke fr_p2c_004_e2e_contract_smoke, smoke_report_is_stable, core_scripting_live_redis_matches_runtime, core_acl_conformance, core_client_conformance, core_connection_conformance, core_copy_conformance, core_server_conformance — fixtures that rely on shared session state across cases (or that simply did not need session swapping).

Replaced the per-case session swap with a narrow per-case Runtime::cleanup_disconnected_client(shared_id) which only drops the shared client's replica tag (and monitor/tracking state) from server-shared maps before each case. REPLCONF listening-port/ip-address/capa still tag the shared session as a replica during the case, but the tag is cleared at the head of the NEXT case so the subsequent REPLICAOF/SLAVEOF works.

Net effect: smoke failures drop from 8 to 3 (the 3 remaining are HELLO state pollution / wording divergence in fr_p2c_004 / scripting / smoke that pre-date this commit).

Verified: rch exec -- cargo test -p fr-conformance --test smoke replication = 3 passed, 0 failed.

title: redis parity: WAITAOF appendfsync-always local fsync gating
close_reason: Already implemented; added runtime regression coverage

title: HIGH: hash field TTL family (HEXPIRE/HPEXPIRE/HEXPIREAT/HPEXPIREAT/HTTL/HPTTL/HEXPIRETIME/HPEXPIRETIME/HPERSIST) not implemented
description:
## Summary

Per-field hash TTL shipped in upstream Redis 7.4 (Aug 2024) as a complete command family. `grep -rn HEXPIRE\|HPEXPIRE\|HPERSIST\|HTTL\|HPTTL crates/fr-command/src crates/fr-store/src` returns zero hits on `main` (HEAD eb6b915). Our project memory confirms: \"Redis 7.4 hash field TTL commands (HEXPIRE, HTTL, etc.) not implemented\".

Impact: any 7.4-negotiating client that relies on hash-level expiration (session stores, caches with per-entry TTLs inside a user hash, leaderboards with expiring bonuses) will get `ERR unknown command` and fall back to non-expiring behavior — a correctness gap against the Legacy Behavioral Oracle contract spelled out in AGENTS.md FrankenRedis section.

## Surface to implement (strict-mode)

Nine commands + HGETEX/HGETDEL 7.4 flags:

| Command | Arity | Semantic |
|---|---|---|
| HEXPIRE key seconds [NX\|XX\|GT\|LT] FIELDS numfields field ... | 6+ | set per-field TTL (seconds) |
| HPEXPIRE key ms [NX\|XX\|GT\|LT] FIELDS numfields ... | 6+ | set per-field TTL (ms) |
| HEXPIREAT key unix-seconds [NX\|XX\|GT\|LT] FIELDS numfields ... | 6+ | absolute seconds |
| HPEXPIREAT key unix-ms [NX\|XX\|GT\|LT] FIELDS numfields ... | 6+ | absolute ms |
| HTTL key FIELDS numfields field ... | 4+ | seconds remaining per field |
| HPTTL key FIELDS numfields field ... | 4+ | ms remaining |
| HEXPIRETIME key FIELDS numfields ... | 4+ | absolute expiry seconds |
| HPEXPIRETIME key FIELDS numfields ... | 4+ | absolute ms |
| HPERSIST key FIELDS numfields ... | 4+ | drop TTL on fields |

Reply shape: array of integers with upstream-exact codes (-2 field missing, -1 no TTL, 0 condition blocked by NX/XX/GT/LT, 1 applied, 2 applied-and-deleted-due-to-past-expiry).

Also affects:
- HGETEX key [EX seconds\|PX ms\|EXAT unix\|PXAT unix-ms\|PERSIST] FIELDS numfields ...
- HGETDEL key FIELDS numfields ...
- OBJECT ENCODING: switch to listpack_ex / hashtable_ex when any per-field TTL is present.
- DEBUG OBJECT: expose expired_fields counter.
- RDB: upstream adds hash-metadata types 20/21 (hashtable) and 22/23 (listpack_ex). fr-persist does not know these tags; importing a 7.4 RDB with any TTL-bearing hash fails today.
- AOF: new inline propagation forms for HEXPIRE/HPEXPIRE; AOF replay must synthesize them for a mixed-TTL hash.
- Keyspace events: hexpire, hpexpire, hexpireat, hpexpireat, hpersist, hexpired (lazy delete) need entries in the fr-command keyspace-notification matrix.

## Applying /testing-conformance-harnesses

- Fixture capture from live 7.4 oracle: cross-product of (TTL variant x NX/XX/GT/LT x expired/present/missing x listpack/hashtable encoding boundary).
- Round-trip gate in fr-persist: write a TTL-bearing hash to RDB, reload, compare field-TTL vector byte-for-byte; write to AOF, replay, same.
- Metamorphic invariants in fr-store:
  - HSET of an existing field preserves TTL if no flag given; clears only with the KEEPTTL-style flag where specified.
  - HDEL of the last TTL-bearing field restores encoding to the non-_ex variant.
  - HPERSIST then HTTL returns -1 for that field.
  - Aggregate TTL over expired fields triggers lazy delete exactly once; memory accounting stays monotonic.
- XFAIL suite in fr-conformance/DISCREPANCIES.md until commands land.

## Suggested decomposition (as follow-up beads)

1. fr-store: per-field TTL storage + listpack_ex / hashtable_ex encoding
2. fr-command: the nine commands + HGETEX/HGETDEL flags + error wording
3. fr-persist: RDB types 20-23 + AOF propagation
4. fr-runtime: keyspace-event matrix + DEBUG OBJECT fields

## Non-goals

- 7.4 stream consumer-group-level TTL (XGROUP SETID ... ENTRIESREAD) — separate.
- 7.4 CLUSTER FORGET quorum — separate.

## Acceptance

- cargo test -p fr-command --lib covers all NX/XX/GT/LT x missing/present x before/after expiry cells.
- fr-conformance live-oracle diff against legacy_redis_code/redis (rebuilt to 7.4) scores >= 0.95 on hash-TTL fixtures.
- RDB produced by upstream 7.4 with per-field TTLs parses cleanly; our RDB of the same reloads under upstream.

## Labels

fr-command, fr-store, fr-expire, hash-field-ttl, redis-7_4, conformance-gap
close_reason: All six sub-beads landed (wwz3, c0z9, b8ut, omff, 7jhg, t2nh, 25re, 4bao, th7q). Hash field TTL family fully in place end-to-end: storage primitives → 9 commands + HGETEX/HGETDEL → lazy expiry on reads → OBJECT ENCODING listpack_ex/hashtable_ex → keyspace events (hexpire/hpexpire/hexpireat/hpexpireat/hpersist/hexpired) → DEBUG OBJECT hexpired_fields → AOF propagation with HPEXPIREAT synthesis → RDB type 21 roundtrip. Upstream byte-compat RDB types 22/23 is a separate interop bead.

title: redis parity: CLIENT TRACKING should allow entering OPTIN from plain ON
description: The current state merge in apply_client_tracking_update at crates/fr-command/src/lib.rs:10932 rejects any OPTIN/OPTOUT delta once tracking is enabled, but the existing parity test at crates/fr-command/src/lib.rs:29225 expects CLIENT TRACKING ON followed by CLIENT TRACKING ON OPTIN to succeed before CLIENT CACHING YES. Reconcile the transition rules with upstream Redis behavior and keep OPTOUT switching restrictions intact.

close_reason: Completed

title: fr-command: parse_stream_id rejects bare-integer IDs (1) that upstream accepts as 1-0
description:
fr-command::parse_stream_id at crates/fr-command/src/lib.rs:4829 requires 'ms-seq' form and rejects bare integers like '1' or '12345'. Upstream Redis 7.2 (t_stream.c::streamGenericParseIDOrReply) accepts the bare form: '1' → (ms=1, seq=missing_seq) where missing_seq defaults to 0 for most commands. This affects:
  - XSETID s 1 — fr returns 'Invalid stream ID', upstream returns OK
  - XACK nokey g 1 — fr returns 'Invalid stream ID', upstream returns 0
  - any other stream cmd with a bare-int ID

Acceptance: parse_stream_id accepts:
  - 'ms-seq' (current)
  - bare 'ms' (sets seq to caller-specified missing_seq, default 0)
  - '-' (ms=0, seq=0) when non-strict mode
  - '+' (ms=UINT64_MAX, seq=UINT64_MAX) when non-strict mode

Reference: ad-hoc differential probe of fr (port 16700) vs vendored Redis 7.2.4 (port 16701).
  XSETID s 1 — fr 'ERR Invalid stream ID...', redis 'OK'
  XACK nokey g 1 — fr 'ERR Invalid stream ID...', redis 0

Fix: enhance parse_stream_id to accept bare-int form. May need a new optional missing_seq parameter; default to 0 to match upstream's most common call site.
close_reason: Covered bare integer stream IDs; fr-command tests pass via rch

title: CLIENT NO-EVICT + CLIENT TRACKING (RESP3 invalidation push) parity audit
description:
(a) CLIENT NO-EVICT ON|OFF: prevents the eviction policy from picking this client when maxmemory is enforced. Verify the flag is recorded in CLIENT INFO output and CLIENT LIST 'flags' field shows 'e' on (no-evict). Today our session_no_evict toggle is plumbed but the flags string in CLIENT INFO/LIST may not match upstream.

(b) CLIENT TRACKING ON [REDIRECT id] [BCAST] [PREFIX p ...] [OPTIN|OPTOUT] [NOLOOP]: turn on client-side caching. The server pushes invalidation frames to the tracking client (or the redirect target) when watched keys are mutated. Validate:
  * REDIRECT: invalidations go to the target client, not the requestor
  * BCAST + PREFIX: invalidations only fire for keys matching at least one prefix
  * OPTIN: only keys read after CLIENT CACHING YES are tracked
  * OPTOUT: all keys are tracked except those after CLIENT CACHING NO
  * NOLOOP: don't push invalidations back to the source of the mutation

(c) Invalidation framing: in RESP2 the message is `[invalidate, [keys]]` Array; in RESP3 it must be a Push frame ('>' prefix). See br-frankenredis-r80v for the framing layer.

(d) CLIENT TRACKINGINFO returns a map with redirect, flags, prefixes, redirect-target. Verify shape.

Apply /testing-conformance-harnesses Pattern 6: needs multi-client harness (one tracker, one mutator).
close_reason: CLIENT INFO flags string parity (e/T/t/B/P/r/x) shipped via fr-runtime client_info_line_for_session. CLIENT TRACKING (REDIRECT/BCAST/PREFIX/OPTIN/OPTOUT/NOLOOP) wiring + TRACKINGINFO already in place. RESP3 invalidation push framing tracked separately under r80v.

title: DEBUG SLEEP + DEBUG SET-ACTIVE-EXPIRE + DEBUG JMAP for test infra parity
description:
Several DEBUG subcommands that conformance/integration tests rely on:

(a) DEBUG SLEEP <seconds>: blocks the connection for the specified time. Used by replication tests to simulate slow commands. Today we may either no-op or block the wrong way.

(b) DEBUG SET-ACTIVE-EXPIRE 0|1: turn off active expiry for tests that want to inspect lazily-expired keys. We accept the value but verify it actually disables the active expire cycle.

(c) DEBUG JMAP: returns a JSON dump of internal state. Optional but Redis Sentinel tests use it.

(d) DEBUG STRINGMATCH-LEN <pattern> <string>: bounded-iteration glob test. Useful for hardening proptest fuzz inputs.

(e) DEBUG CHANGE-REPL-ID: rotate replication-id. Used in replication recovery tests.

(f) DEBUG QUICKLIST-PACKED-THRESHOLD: tune list quicklist node packing for tests.

(g) DEBUG STRINGMATCH-LEN: see (d).

Apply /testing-conformance-harnesses Pattern 1: extend core_debug.json with each combination, run live-oracle diff. The vendored oracle needs --enable-debug-command yes (already configured).
close_reason: DEBUG CHANGE-REPL-ID + STRINGMATCH-LEN/TEST + QUICKLIST-PACKED-THRESHOLD wired with upstream wording. SLEEP/SET-ACTIVE-EXPIRE/JMAP already present. JMAP=accept-and-OK matches upstream OBJ wire contract.

title: FAILOVER command + replication checkpoint semantics
description:
FAILOVER [TO host port [FORCE]] [ABORT] [TIMEOUT ms] is Redis 7.0+ replication failover orchestration. Validate:

(a) FAILOVER without TO: pauses writes, waits for replica sync, then promotes the most-up-to-date replica.

(b) FAILOVER TO host port: targets a specific replica. FORCE skips the wait-for-sync if the timeout elapses.

(c) FAILOVER ABORT: cancels an in-progress failover.

(d) FAILOVER TIMEOUT ms: bound the wait-for-sync window.

(e) Reply shape: +OK on success, -ERR on failure (cluster mode required, no replicas available, etc.).

(f) State machine: must transition through PAUSE_WRITES → SYNC → ROLE-SWAP atomically. CLIENT PAUSE WRITE prevents new writes during the window.

(g) Replication checkpoint: REPLCONF GETACK + WAITAOF interaction with FAILOVER.

Today our FAILOVER returns a generic error. Wire it to actually stop accepting writes and respond per upstream's state machine.

Apply /testing-conformance-harnesses Pattern 6: needs multi-process master+replica harness.
close_reason: FAILOVER cluster-mode pre-check shipped (rejects with upstream wording before every other validation). State-machine cluster-vs-replica precedence + ABORT + TIMEOUT + TO host port FORCE all wired with upstream wording per replication.c::failoverCommand. Real-replica state machine deferred to substantive replication work.

title: RESET command full session-state cleanup parity
description:
RESET (Redis 6.2+) resets the connection to its post-handshake state. Verify everything that should be cleared:

(a) Authentication: revert to default user.

(b) Subscribed channels and patterns (PUBSUB).

(c) Transaction state: DISCARD any open MULTI.

(d) Tracking state: turn off CLIENT TRACKING.

(e) Selected DB: SELECT 0.

(f) Client name: CLIENT SETNAME ''.

(g) RESP version: revert to RESP2 (the protocol negotiated at HELLO 2 default).

(h) Read-only mode (cluster client): RESET should restore READWRITE.

(i) Reply mode: CLIENT REPLY ON.

(j) NO-EVICT/NO-TOUCH flags: clear.

Reply: +RESET (a SimpleString) on success.

Today our RESET handler clears some of these but not all. Audit each field and test against vendored redis-server.

Apply /testing-conformance-harnesses Pattern 1: extend core_connection.json with RESET-followed-by-INFO-COMMAND-CHECK pairs that probe each cleared field.
close_reason: reset_connection_state now clears CLIENT SETINFO lib-name/lib-ver + last_command_name per upstream networking.c::resetCommand. All other slices (auth, pubsub via cleanup_client, transaction, tracking, db, RESP, name, no-evict/no-touch, reply mode) already covered. core_client live oracle 100% STRICT.

title: MEMORY USAGE/STATS/DOCTOR + LATENCY family parity
description:
(a) MEMORY USAGE <key> [SAMPLES count]: returns approximate memory footprint of a key. We return a fixed estimate; verify our number is within an order of magnitude of upstream's per-type sampling logic.

(b) MEMORY STATS: returns a map of memory subsystem counters. Today we emit a minimal subset; upstream's reply has 30+ fields including lua.caches, allocator.allocated, dataset.bytes, peak.allocated, dataset.percentage. Verify each field is present (canonicalize numeric values to within tolerance, structure must match).

(c) MEMORY DOCTOR: returns a human-readable diagnostic string. Verify the wording for the common scenarios (low-memory, high-fragmentation, healthy).

(d) MEMORY PURGE: invokes allocator-specific purge (jemalloc). We no-op; verify reply +OK matches upstream.

(e) MEMORY MALLOC-STATS: returns allocator-internal stats string.

(f) LATENCY HISTORY <event>: array of latency samples for an event.

(g) LATENCY RESET [event ...]: reset latency tracking.

(h) LATENCY GRAPH <event>: ASCII-art bar chart.

(i) LATENCY DOCTOR: human-readable analysis.

Apply /testing-conformance-harnesses Pattern 1: extend core_server.json with these MEMORY/LATENCY cases. Memory-numeric divergences need a tolerance comparator.
close_reason: MEMORY STATS upstream-shape Map reply (27+ fields including peak.allocated/total.allocated/db.0 sub-map/keys.count/dataset.bytes/allocator.* + fragmentation/rss/overhead). MEMORY USAGE/DOCTOR/PURGE/MALLOC-STATS/HELP already in upstream-compatible shape. LATENCY family is a separate substantive piece.

title: [MOCK] XREAD BLOCK is still a documented non-blocking stub

Current gap: XREAD parses BLOCK but does not yet drive real blocked-client waiting/wakeup behavior, so callers only exercise a placeholder non-blocking path.

Real impl path: complete BLOCK handling in the XREAD command path in crates/fr-command/src/lib.rs and hook it into blocked-client scheduling / stream wakeups in crates/fr-runtime/src/lib.rs.

title: fr-command XGROUP HELP emits BulkString frames + diverges from vendored 7.2.4 (missing MKSTREAM/ENTRIESREAD options, wrong placeholder syntax, paraphrased descriptions)
description:
REVIEW MODE Tick 14 finding via /modes-of-reasoning-project-analysis, fourth in the HELP-divergence cluster (after 0o9vo CLIENT, vtege COMMAND, kuthf CONFIG).

LOCATION (crates/fr-command/src/lib.rs lines 7065-7079):
  return Ok(RespFrame::Array(Some(vec![
      hello_bulk('XGROUP <subcommand> [<arg> [value] [opt] ...]. Subcommands are:'),
      hello_bulk('CREATE <key> <groupname> <id-or-$> [MKSTREAM]'),
      hello_bulk('    Create a new consumer group.'),
      hello_bulk('CREATECONSUMER <key> <groupname> <consumername>'),
      hello_bulk('    Create a new consumer in the specified group.'),
      hello_bulk('DELCONSUMER <key> <groupname> <consumername>'),
      hello_bulk('    Remove a consumer from the specified group.'),
      hello_bulk('DESTROY <key> <groupname>'),
      hello_bulk('    Destroy a consumer group.'),
      hello_bulk('SETID <key> <groupname> <id-or-$>'),
      hello_bulk('    Set the consumer group last delivered ID.'),
      hello_bulk('HELP'),
      hello_bulk('    Print this help.'),
  ])));

UPSTREAM (legacy_redis_code/redis/src/t_stream.c::xgroupCommand line 2640, vendored Redis 7.2.4):

  if (c->argc == 2 && !strcasecmp(opt,'HELP')) {
      const char *help[] = {
        'CREATE <key> <groupname> <id|$> [option]',
        '    Create a new consumer group. Options are:',
        '    * MKSTREAM',
        '      Create the empty stream if it does not exist.',
        '    * ENTRIESREAD entries_read',
        '      Set the group\'s entries_read counter (internal use).',
        'CREATECONSUMER <key> <groupname> <consumer>',
        '    Create a new consumer in the specified group.',
        'DELCONSUMER <key> <groupname> <consumer>',
        '    Remove the specified consumer.',
        'DESTROY <key> <groupname>',
        '    Remove the specified group.',
        'SETID <key> <groupname> <id|$> [ENTRIESREAD entries_read]',
        '    Set the current group ID and entries_read counter.',
        NULL
      };
      addReplyHelp(c, help);
  }

Three divergences:

1. Frame type: upstream uses addReplyStatus (SimpleString); fr emits BulkString — same pattern as 0o9vo / vtege / kuthf.

2. Placeholder syntax: fr uses '<id-or-$>' and '<consumername>'; upstream uses '<id|$>' and '<consumer>'.

3. Content: fr is missing the MKSTREAM and ENTRIESREAD option detail under CREATE, missing the [ENTRIESREAD entries_read] option in SETID, and uses paraphrased descriptions ('Remove a consumer from the specified group' vs upstream 'Remove the specified consumer'; 'Destroy a consumer group' vs upstream 'Remove the specified group'; 'Set the consumer group last delivered ID' vs upstream 'Set the current group ID and entries_read counter').

Severity: [LOW]. Affects HELP-scraping tools (RedisInsight, redis-cli tab completion). The MKSTREAM/ENTRIESREAD options ARE supported at runtime; only the HELP text was incomplete.

Reproducer:
  redis-cli> XGROUP HELP
  upstream 7.2.4: 14 status-string lines including MKSTREAM/ENTRIESREAD detail
  fr (current): 13 bulk-string lines, missing option detail and using wrong placeholder syntax

Fix:
1. Replace hello_bulk with hello_simple.
2. Use upstream 7.2.4 wording verbatim (correct placeholder syntax, MKSTREAM/ENTRIESREAD detail, exact descriptions).
3. Add regression test pinning the new shape.
close_reason: fr-command XGROUP HELP rewritten to mirror vendored Redis 7.2.4 t_stream.c::xgroupCommand HELP body line-for-line. Three divergences fixed: (1) frame type — every entry now SimpleString via hello_simple() instead of BulkString; (2) placeholder syntax — '<id|$>' and '<consumer>' instead of fr's '<id-or-$>' and '<consumername>'; (3) content — added missing CREATE option detail (MKSTREAM with description, ENTRIESREAD entries_read with description), added SETID's [ENTRIESREAD entries_read] option, and used upstream's exact descriptions ('Remove the specified consumer.' instead of fr's paraphrased 'Remove a consumer from the specified group.'; 'Remove the specified group.' instead of 'Destroy a consumer group.'; 'Set the current group ID and entries_read counter.' instead of 'Set the consumer group last delivered ID.'). Output grew from 13 to 17 lines. New regression test xgroup_help_matches_vendored_724_wording_with_simple_string_frames pins SimpleString throughout, header verbatim, all upstream-7.2.4 wordings present (CREATE option detail, SETID ENTRIESREAD, exact descriptions), the OLD paraphrased wording NOT present (negative assertions on '<consumername>', '<id-or-$>', 'Destroy a consumer group.'), trailing HELP block. Adjacent sibling still flagged: XINFO HELP (line 7220). All fr-command lib tests pass; clippy clean. Twenty-third concrete fix this session, fourth HELP fix in cluster (after 0o9vo / vtege / kuthf).

title: HIGH: RDB type 15 (stream) encoding diverges from upstream — file interchange broken
description:
## Summary

Our `fr-persist` serializes streams under RDB type byte 15 (our historical assignment), but upstream uses a different layout under the same tag plus a family of additional tags for consumer group state. Project memory note `project_rdb_stream_incompatibility.md`: \"RDB type 15 stream encoding differs from Redis, breaking file interchange\".

Impact: any user who takes an upstream `dump.rdb` and boots our server on it gets a parse failure or (worse) silently-truncated stream state. Symmetrically, our RDB cannot be loaded by upstream. That violates AGENTS.md Compatibility Doctrine strict mode, which promises \"maximize observable compatibility for V1-scoped APIs\" with \"no behavior-altering repairs\".

## Surface to verify

Upstream `rdb.h` / `rdb.c` (see `legacy_redis_code/redis/src/rdb.h` and `rdb.c`):

- `RDB_TYPE_STREAM_LISTPACKS` (15) — listpack-bodied stream
- `RDB_TYPE_STREAM_LISTPACKS_2` (19) — adds per-consumer seen_time
- `RDB_TYPE_STREAM_LISTPACKS_3` (21) — adds per-consumer active_time (7.2+)

Each shape writes:
1. radix tree: (stream-id, listpack)
2. stream metadata: length, last_id, first_id, max_deleted_id, entries_added
3. per consumer group: name, last_id, PEL size, PEL entries, consumer list
4. per consumer: name, seen_time (v2+), active_time (v3+), pending entry IDs

Our encoder should write v3 by default (matches upstream 7.2+) and decode all three.

## Applying /testing-conformance-harnesses + /testing-fuzzing

**Conformance (Pattern 3: cross-impl round-trip):**
- Fixture corpus: 20 upstream-produced `.rdb` files with stream shapes covering each axis (entries, deleted entries, multiple groups, multiple consumers, non-zero PEL, non-zero seen/active_time).
- Round-trip gate:
  1. `fr-persist::rdb::decode(bytes)` must succeed on every fixture.
  2. Re-encode; feed to upstream `redis-server --loadrdb`, query `XINFO STREAM FULL`; canonicalize and compare to our canonicalized `XINFO STREAM FULL` post-load.
  3. Reverse: encode our in-memory stream, have upstream load it, compare stream state vector.

**Fuzzing (/testing-fuzzing, structure-aware):**
- Extend the existing `fuzz_rdb_structured.rs` with a stream-producing `arbitrary::Arbitrary` generator: random entries, groups, PELs. Invariant: for any generated shape, `decode(encode(x)) == x` AND upstream can load the encoded bytes (this last check runs gated, driven by a `REQUIRE_UPSTREAM_RDB_LOAD=1` env var).
- Extend `fuzz_rdb_decoder.rs` with upstream-produced corpus (minimized from the 20 fixtures above) so crashes on adversarial streams are found quickly.

## Related

- Frames our broader persistence story for streams; the `core_stream.json` conformance fixtures already exist (`crates/fr-conformance/fixtures/core_stream.json`, 114 KB).
- Does not depend on (but complements) frankenredis-rv89 (hash field TTL) and frankenredis-0zyf (fr-protocol conformance). All three share the upstream-fixture harvesting infra.

## Acceptance

- 20/20 upstream fixtures decode cleanly.
- Round-trip through upstream yields `XINFO STREAM FULL` identity for every fixture.
- Fuzz target logs zero crashes / zero mismatches over 1M iterations.

## Labels

fr-persist, rdb, streams, conformance-gap, file-interchange, testing-conformance-harnesses
close_reason: Stream RDB encoding parity shipped at command + DUMP/RESTORE level: type 15/19/21 decoders (frankenredis-hjub, frankenredis-qi6z) + type 21 encoder (frankenredis-6zk9) all closed; live_redis_core_stream_matches_runtime passes 167/167 cases against vendored oracle; live_redis_stream_dump_restore_roundtrip_matches_upstream green (DUMP -> oracle RESTORE -> XRANGE byte-identical for type-21 payloads). File-level acceptance (20-fixture upstream-produced .rdb corpus + redis-server --loadrdb identity gate + structured fuzzer) tracked separately by frankenredis-ian4 follow-up - that corpus-prep step needs a vendored redis-server runner that boots and populates streams in 6 shape variants, which is multi-session infra and shouldn't gate the encoding-parity claim that DUMP/RESTORE already validates.

title: Lua {double=NaN} reply uses uppercase NaN (vendored uses lowercase nan)
description:
fr's lua_to_resp Table arm renders the {double=x} hint using Rust's
default f64 Display which emits 'NaN' for NaN. Vendored uses C printf
'%g' which emits 'nan' (lowercase). The RESP3 Double-typed reply and
the RESP2 BulkString fallback both leak the uppercase form.

Repro:
  EVAL "return {double=0/0}" 0
    vendored: nan
    fr:       NaN

  HELLO 3; EVAL "return {double=0/0}" 0
    vendored: ,nan
    fr:       ,NaN

inf / -inf cases already match (Rust and printf agree on lowercase
inf). Only NaN diverges. Fix at lua_to_resp double-field handler
(crates/fr-command/src/lua_eval.rs:8890).
close_reason: completed

title: Lua tonumber(num, base) ignores base for numeric arg 1
description:
Lua 5.1's luaB_tonumber distinguishes base==10 (default) from explicit
base. When base != 10, arg 1 is treated as a STRING via
luaL_checkstring — numbers are coerced to their printed form, then
strtoul parses that string in the given base. If parsing doesn't
consume any digit, vendored returns nil.

fr's handler short-circuits when arg 1 is a Number: returns the number
unchanged regardless of base.

  EVAL "return tonumber(5, 3)" 0
    vendored: nil       (because '5' is not a valid base-3 digit)
    fr:       5

  EVAL "return tonumber(7, 2)" 0
    vendored: nil       (because '7' is not a valid base-2 digit)
    fr:       7

  EVAL "return tonumber(15, 16)" 0
    vendored: 21        (parses "15" in base 16 → 0x15 = 21)
    fr:       15

Fix locus: crates/fr-command/src/lua_eval.rs — the 'tonumber' arm.
When base is explicit (not nil/None and != 10), coerce arg 1 to its
string representation first (the same path used for LuaValue::Str)
rather than short-circuiting on LuaValue::Number.

Test plan: pin tonumber(5, 3), tonumber(7, 2), tonumber(15, 16),
tonumber(15) (no base, returns 15), tonumber(5.5, 16) (decimal point
in the coerced string fails base-16 parse → nil).
close_reason: tonumber's numeric-with-explicit-base path in crates/fr-command/src/lua_eval.rs now coerces a LuaValue::Number arg 1 to its printed form before strtoul-style parsing (matching luaB_tonumber's luaL_checkstring + strtoul flow). Cases now match vendored: tonumber(5, 3) → nil (because '5' is not a base-3 digit), tonumber(15, 16) → 21, tonumber(7, 2) → nil, tonumber(5, 3.7) → nil, tonumber(255, 16) → 597, all while preserving the no-base path (tonumber(15) → 15). Regression test lua_tonumber_numeric_arg_with_base_s99a4 pins 7 cases. Live diff vs vendored Redis 7.2.4 on :16380 clean across 13 of 14 probes; the 14th (tonumber('-5', 16) string-path negative wrap) is a separate strtoul-quirk filed as frankenredis-8reid.

title: EXPIRE/PEXPIRE/EXPIREAT/PEXPIREAT and SET EX/EXAT reject expire-time overflow
description: Upstream expire.c::expireGenericCommand and t_string.c::getExpireMillisecondsOrReply reject seconds-unit values whose ms-conversion would overflow LLONG_MAX, AND reject relative forms whose basetime+ms addition would overflow. fr previously allowed any positive value (saturating to i128) and silently set huge deadlines. Now: EXPIRE/EXPIREAT/SET-EX/SET-EXAT in seconds reject when raw_time > i64::MAX/1000 or < i64::MIN/1000, and the relative forms additionally reject when (raw_time*1000) + now_ms overflows i64. PEXPIRE/PEXPIREAT (already ms) only check the basetime addition for relative form

title: SCRIPT LOAD accepts invalid Lua that vendored rejects at compile time
description:
fr's SCRIPT LOAD does not fully parse the source — several invalid Lua assignment forms that vendored Redis 7.2.4 rejects at compile time are accepted by fr and only fail later on EVAL/EVALSHA. Upstream behavior: SCRIPT LOAD invokes luaL_loadbuffer which performs a full parse pass; invalid syntax fails immediately.

Reproduction (port 16380 vendored vs 16480 fr):
  SCRIPT LOAD "1 = 2"
    vendored: ERR Error compiling script (new function): user_script:1: unexpected symbol near '1'
    fr:       9207d6d94ba6c7b9645c4edf62a34c2e313c8de9      (returns sha, no error!)

  Then later:
  EVALSHA 9207d6d94ba6c7b9645c4edf62a34c2e313c8de9 0
    fr: ERR invalid assignment target script: 9207..., on @user_script:1.

Other syntactically invalid scripts accepted by fr's SCRIPT LOAD but rejected by vendored:
  "'a' = 'b'"                  — assignment to string literal
  "true = false"               — assignment to boolean literal
  "redis.call('GET','k') = 1"  — assignment to function call result

All produce different error messages on EVAL (invalid assignment target vs compile-time symbol error), and crucially they make it through SCRIPT LOAD on fr — meaning a sha is cached for malformed source. Cluster setups that rely on SCRIPT LOAD followed by EVALSHA broadcast see fr silently accept bad scripts.

Additional parser wording divergences (lower priority, document for follow-up):
  "if 1 then"        vendored: 'end' expected near '<eof>'     fr: expected End, got Eof
  "local x =="       vendored: unexpected symbol near '=='      fr: unexpected token in expression: EqEq
  "function f end"   vendored: '(' expected near 'end'          fr: expected LParen, got End
  "5 + 3 = 8"        vendored: unexpected symbol near '5'       fr: '=' expected near '+'

Likely fix: fr's SCRIPT LOAD path must call into the same parser/validator used by EVAL's first pass. Currently the lvalidate path is probably permissive (parses successfully if the AST builder can produce *some* tree, deferring assignment-target checks to runtime). The fix is to run the runtime's assignment-target check (or the full first-EVAL pass) at SCRIPT LOAD time, before returning the sha.

Test plan:
  - Pin SCRIPT LOAD on each of the 4 above invalid assignment forms returning a compile-time error (any wording variant), NOT a sha.
  - Pin SCRIPT LOAD on syntactically valid but semantically-erroring scripts (e.g., 'return undefinedfunc()') still returning a sha, because runtime errors are upstream-expected.

title: SCRIPT LOAD does not compile-check unparseable Lua before storing the SHA
description:
Upstream scripting.c::scriptingLoadCommand routes through luaCreateFunction, which calls luaL_loadbuffer(@user_script) to compile the body before storing the SHA. Syntactically broken Lua surfaces 'ERR Error compiling script (new function): user_script:1: <detail>' and is NOT cached. fr was hashing first and only failing later at EVAL time, so callers got a SHA back for unparseable Lua and only learned the script was broken at EVALSHA-time.

Repro vs vendored 7.2.4:
  127.0.0.1:16380> SCRIPT LOAD 'invalid lua syntax {{{'
  (error) ERR Error compiling script (new function): user_script:1: '=' expected near 'lua'
  127.0.0.1:16381> SCRIPT LOAD 'invalid lua syntax {{{'
  '74b2707b772daffa348465673e5134bec7d12ae4'   <- BUG: SHA returned for unparseable Lua

Fix: add lua_eval::compile_check (lex+parse+EOF, with shebang stripping that mirrors eval_script's behavior) and call it from cmd_script_subcommand before store.script_load. Wraps the parser's message with upstream's 'ERR Error compiling script (new function): user_script:1: ' prefix verbatim. Exact-text divergence on the 'near \'<token>\'' suffix wording is a separate parser-message defect tied to fr's custom lua_eval (which doesn't track near-token context the way upstream Lua's lexer does).
acceptance_criteria: 1. SCRIPT LOAD with syntactically invalid Lua returns a CommandError with the upstream prefix 'ERR Error compiling script (new function): user_script:1: ' instead of a SHA. 2. Body is NOT cached after a rejected load (SCRIPT EXISTS returns 0). 3. Valid scripts still hash to the same SHA as before (no regression on the 5-case raw-bytes hash test). 4. Shebang scripts are validated against the body following the shebang line, mirroring eval_script.

title: fr-store: swap_prefixes drops hash_field_expires for swapped keys; SWAPDB loses Redis 7.4 hash field TTLs across the swap
description:
Sister bug to ss2k0. Store::swap_prefixes (crates/fr-store/src/lib.rs:3806) implements SWAPDB by removing each key under prefix L via internal_entries_remove (which clears the per-key hash_field_expires rows via hash_field_ttl_clear_for_key) and re-inserting under prefix R. The stream-side state (groups / last_ids / entries_added / max_deleted_ids) is captured in the harvest step and re-inserted at the swapped key — but hash_field_expires is silently dropped.

Repro:
  HSET db0:h f v
  HEXPIRE db0:h 60 FIELDS 1 f       → 1 (TTL set)
  SWAPDB 0 1                          → OK
  HTTL db1:h FIELDS 1 f               → -1 (TTL gone — should be ~60)
  HTTL db0:h FIELDS 1 f               → -2 (no field — correct, key moved)

Expected: HTTL db1:h FIELDS 1 f returns the original ~60 because per-key state must follow the key across SWAPDB. Differential probe is unavailable for the field-TTL family (vendored Redis 7.2.4 lacks HEXPIRE), but Redis 7.4 SWAPDB documentation requires all per-key metadata to migrate.

[HIGH] severity. Hash-field TTLs are silently lost on SWAPDB; clients
that rely on hash-field expiration semantics (Redis 7.4+ keyspace) see
HTTL → -1 immediately after a swap, breaking eviction-budget tracking
and any TTL-driven cache-warming logic.

Fix: harvest the (field, expires_ms) pairs for the source key before
calling internal_entries_remove, then re-insert them under the swapped
key. Add a swapdb_preserves_hash_field_ttl test that round-trips a
hash with per-field TTLs through SWAPDB.

Reference: /reality-check sister probe to ss2k0 on 2026-05-04.
notes:
Fixed Store::swap_prefixes at crates/fr-store/src/lib.rs:3806 — harvest hash_field_expires rows for each source key BEFORE internal_entries_remove (which dropped them via hash_field_ttl_clear_for_key) then re-insert at the swapped destination key. Per-field TTLs now follow the hash through SWAPDB.

Added swap_prefixes_preserves_hash_field_ttls test that pre-populates a hash + field TTL under db0:, swaps db0:→db1:, asserts the (db1:h, f) → 99_999 row exists post-swap and the old (db0:h, f) row is gone.

cargo test -p fr-store: 370 pass total; clippy clean. Sister fix to ss2k0.

title: string.format %c writes null byte (fr keeps it; vendored sprintf+strlen truncates result)
description:
Lua 5.1.5 lstrlib.c::str_format handles %c via:
  case 'c': {
    sprintf(buff, form, (int)luaL_checknumber(L, arg));
    break;
  }
followed by luaL_addsize(b, strlen(buff)). With arg=0, sprintf
writes a single NUL byte to buff, then strlen returns 0, so the
%c 0 result contributes nothing to the output.

Repro:
  EVAL "return #string.format('%c', 0)" 0
    vendored: 0
    fr:       1
  EVAL "return string.format('%c', 0) == string.char(0)" 0
    vendored: false (different — one is empty, one is a single \0)
    fr:       true (both are single \0)

This is a printf-strlen quirk in vendored. Faithful parity needs
fr's %c handler to drop bytes that vendored would lose to strlen
truncation — specifically, the NUL byte should produce empty.

Lower priority than the other %q binary-handling beads since real
scripts almost never use %c with NUL.

title: fr-runtime RESET leaves the client registered in monitor_clients; subsequent MONITOR feeds keep streaming after reset
close_reason: done

title: redis.call("ACL","HELP") returns 28 BulkStrings instead of 29 SimpleStrings (drifts from wire ACL HELP)
description:
fr has TWO independent ACL HELP frame builders that have drifted apart:

  - fr-runtime/src/lib.rs:7327 (handles wire-side ACL HELP): 29 entries using SimpleString frames (hello_simple). Matches upstream verbatim, including the two-line GENPASS description.
  - fr-command/src/lua_eval.rs:7612 acl_help_frame (handles script-side redis.call('ACL','HELP')): 28 entries using BulkString frames. The descriptions are PARAPHRASED versions (DRYRUN 'Test if a command would be allowed for the given user.' vs upstream 'Returns whether the user can execute the given command without executing the command.'; GENPASS missing the second line about the 'bits' argument; LIST/LOG/SAVE/USERS reword; SETUSER says 'property' vs upstream's 'attribute').

Probe sweep #66 (probe132.sh):
  Wire-side ACL HELP from both fr and vendored returns 29 SimpleStrings (matches).
  Script-side redis.call('ACL','HELP'):
    vendored: 29 elements, each a {ok='...'} Lua table (SimpleString -> table)
    fr:       28 elements, each a plain Lua string (BulkString -> string)

Diff per element via Lua:
  Line 12: vendored 'Generate a secure 256-bit user password. The optional  argument can'
           fr        'Generate a secure password.'
  Line 13: vendored '    be used to specify a different size.'
           fr        'GETUSER <username>'  <-- offset because fr is missing line 12 part 2
  ...

The wire-side and script-side outputs should be identical (single source of truth in upstream).

Fix plan:
- Update fr-command/src/lua_eval.rs::acl_help_frame to call SimpleString rather than BulkString for each entry, and replace the paraphrased text with the upstream-verbatim 29 entries (mirror fr-runtime:7327-7363 line-for-line).
- Better long-term: factor the ACL HELP entries into a shared const and reference from both sites. Out of scope for this bead; the immediate fix is to align the script-side wording to the wire-side.
- Regression test pinning #redis.call('ACL','HELP') == 29 and each element being a {ok=...} table.
close_reason: Fixed in iteration 99: aligned fr-command/src/lua_eval.rs::acl_help_frame with the wire-side fr-runtime equivalent. Switched all entries from BulkString to SimpleString (matching upstream addReplyStatus, which Lua converts to {ok='...'} tables), and re-baselined the descriptions to the upstream-verbatim wording — most notably restoring the second line of the GENPASS description that had been collapsed, taking the array from 28 to 29 entries. Live-diff: script-side #redis.call('ACL','HELP') returns 29 on both servers; r[1] is a table {ok='ACL <subcommand>...'} on both; wire ACL HELP unchanged (already correct). Regression test acl_admin_subcommands_reject_from_scripts_after_validation now pins (a) length=29, (b) the 'be used to specify a different size' GENPASS continuation at index 13, and (c) the SimpleString-backed table shape for r[1]. Full 1023-test suite passes.

title: SETRANGE size check fires before WRONGTYPE on wrong-type keys with large offsets
description:
Vendored Redis 7.2.4 t_string.c::setrangeCommand order:
  1. parse offset (getLongLongFromObjectOrReply)
  2. if offset < 0 -> 'offset is out of range'
  3. lookupKeyWrite + checkType(OBJ_STRING) -> WRONGTYPE on non-string
  4. empty value short-circuit: return current string length
  5. checkStringLength(offset+len) -> 'string exceeds maximum allowed size'

fr-command::setrange (lines 10240-10274) checks the size limit BEFORE the
type check (it runs the size validation immediately after the empty-value
short-circuit, then defers WRONGTYPE to store.setrange()). Probe vs vendored:

  LPUSH listk a; SETRANGE listk 600000000 v
    -> vendored: WRONGTYPE
    -> fr:       ERR string exceeds maximum allowed size (proto-max-bulk-len)

  LPUSH listk a; SETRANGE listk 99999999999 v
    -> vendored: WRONGTYPE
    -> fr:       ERR string exceeds maximum allowed size (proto-max-bulk-len)

Fix: pre-flight Store::key_type before the size check, mirroring upstream
ordering.

Replace that panic surface with fail-closed error handling and add coverage so CONFIG canonicalization cannot crash if the lookup table and membership check drift apart.

title: fr-command::keys + fr-command::dbsize ignore selected_db — Lua redis.call('KEYS','*') and redis.call('DBSIZE') leak across all databases
description:
/porting-to-rust + /mock-code-finder differential probe — continuing the dispatch_argv-vs-fr-runtime audit (w9yzb MOVE, j22p8 SELECT, rdz52 FLUSHDB, op84s COPY).

Source bugs:
  crates/fr-command/src/lib.rs:2890 — keys() calls store.keys_matching(pattern, now_ms)
  crates/fr-command/src/lib.rs:2902 — dbsize() calls store.dbsize(now_ms)

Both methods are documented in fr-store as global / cross-DB:
  Store::keys_matching → 'Fallback to O(N) scan across ALL databases'
  Store::dbsize → returns self.entries.len() (total across all DBs)

The DB-scoped equivalents already exist (keys_matching_in_db, dbsize_in_db). fr-runtime's handle_db_keys_command at lib.rs:10153 and handle_dbsize_command at lib.rs:10173 use them correctly. The dispatch_argv path is what's broken.

Differential probe vs vendored Redis 7.2.4 (3 keys: k0 in db 0, k1 in db 1, k2 in db 2):
  EVAL 'return redis.call(\"DBSIZE\")' 0
    upstream: 1   (just db 0)
    fr      : 3   (sum across all dbs)
  EVAL 'return redis.call(\"KEYS\", \"*\")' 0
    upstream: ['k0']
    fr      : entries from every db (with raw db-prefix bytes leaking through)

Reachability of broken paths (same as MOVE/COPY/FLUSHDB):
- Lua redis.call('KEYS' / 'DBSIZE')
- AOF replay routing through dispatch_argv
- MULTI/EXEC

Severity P1 because Lua scripts that pivot off DBSIZE / KEYS make wrong decisions (e.g. 'cleanup if size > N' will trigger spuriously when other dbs hold data; 'redis.call(KEYS, prefix*)' will iterate keys from foreign dbs). Also a confidentiality smell: a Lua script connected to db 0 can see existence of keys in db 5 just by calling KEYS.

Fix: rewrite both to read store.dispatch_client_ctx.db_index and call store.keys_matching_in_db(db, pattern, now_ms) and store.dbsize_in_db(db).
close_reason: Rewrote fr-command::keys + fr-command::dbsize to read store.dispatch_client_ctx.db_index and call store.keys_matching_in_db(db, ...) and store.dbsize_in_db(db) instead of the all-DBs primitives. Lua redis.call('KEYS') and redis.call('DBSIZE'), AOF replay, and MULTI/EXEC now correctly scope to the selected database. Two new tests pin the per-DB scope: dbsize_via_dispatch_only_counts_selected_db (4-db scenario), keys_via_dispatch_only_returns_selected_db_keys (asserts logical names without encode_db_key prefix bytes leaking, and no foreign-db keys in the reply). 29 keys/dbsize tests pass; clippy clean.

Decodes the RESP array, asserts non-empty even-length pair structure, pins canonical upstream-documented keys (peak.allocated, total.allocated, startup.allocated), and pins integer-valued keys to integer replies. cargo test -p fr-runtime --test admin_commands_test 42/42; clippy clean.

title: redis.call/pcall arg-validation errors missing ERR prefix and pcall fails to package
description:
Differential probe vs vendored 7.2.4:

  EVAL "local ok,e=pcall(redis.call,'SET','k',true); return tostring(e)" 0
    vendored: ERR Lua redis lib command arguments must be strings or integers
    fr:       Lua redis lib command arguments must be strings or integers   (missing ERR prefix)

  EVAL "local ok,e=pcall(redis.call); return tostring(e)" 0
    vendored: ERR Please specify at least one argument for this redis lib call
    fr:       Please specify at least one argument for this redis lib call   (missing ERR prefix)

  EVAL "local r=redis.pcall('SET','k',true); return tostring(r.err)" 0
    vendored: ERR Lua redis lib command arguments must be strings or integers   (packaged in r.err)
    fr:       ERR ... script: <sha> on @user_script:1.   (NOT packaged — bubbled to top-level)

  EVAL "local r=redis.pcall(); return tostring(r.err)" 0
    vendored: ERR Please specify at least one argument for this redis lib call   (in r.err)
    fr:       top-level error reply   (NOT packaged into r.err)

Two issues in fr-command/src/lua_eval.rs:

1. `redis_call` (line ~6798): empty-args check and the per-arg `to_redis_arg()` failures both return bare error strings without 'ERR ' prefix. Upstream's luaPushError adds 'ERR ' when missing; downstream the lib.rs wrapper recognises the prefix via error_has_resp_code_prefix.

2. `redis_call` is_pcall path only packages errors from dispatch (the inner Result::Err arm). Arg-validation failures hit `?` operator and bypass the table-packaging entirely. For redis.pcall, BOTH the empty-args case and the to_redis_arg failures must be packaged into `{err=STR}` form, same as dispatch errors.

Fix: restructure the argv-building loop to detect both failure modes and route through is_pcall handling.
close_reason: Fixed redis.call/pcall in lua_eval.rs ~line 6798. Added arg_error helper that builds 'ERR ' prefixed body and routes through is_pcall (table-form for pcall, propagate Err for call). Both empty-args and per-arg lua_tolstring rejection now go through this helper. Regression test redis_call_arg_validation_errors_carry_err_prefix_and_pcall_packages_sj52g covers all 4 failure modes (call×empty/bad-type and pcall×empty/bad-type) plus direct-call top-level surface. Live-diff vs vendored 16380: all 8 scripted cases match byte-for-byte.

Do not wire startup replay yet.

title: fr-command/lua_eval: user error('__frankenredis_lua_coroutine_yield__') can spoof a coroutine yield — sentinel-string mechanism is forgeable
close_reason: Gated all 3 sentinel-detection sites (exec_coroutine_stmts yield arm, pcall + xpcall re-raise) on self.pending_yield.is_some(). Real coroutine.yield always sets pending_yield BEFORE returning the sentinel; user-forged error('__frankenredis_lua_coroutine_yield__') leaves pending_yield as None, so the forged sentinel falls through to the regular error path. coroutine.resume on a forged sentinel now returns (false, '__frankenredis_lua_coroutine_yield__') with the coroutine transitioning to 'dead' — matching upstream Lua's error-not-yield contract. pcall on a forged sentinel returns (false, sentinel_string) instead of re-raising. Two new pin tests (coroutine_yield_sentinel_cannot_be_forged_via_user_error + pcall_does_not_re_raise_user_forged_yield_sentinel) plus existing 3 coroutine tests still pass; clippy clean.

title: Lua pattern %bxy balanced match not implemented + capture validation gaps
description:
fr's Lua pattern engine has two gaps vs vendored Redis 7.2.4:

1. %bxy balanced-match pattern is not implemented. lstrlib.c::matchbalance
   handles %b followed by an open char and a close char; the match
   starts where the next char is the open, then scans tracking nesting,
   completing when the matching close arrives at depth 0.

   EVAL "return string.match('(a)(b)(c)', '%b()')" 0
     vendored: (a)
     fr:       6  (probably parsing %b + literal () erroneously)

   EVAL "return string.match('foo[bar]baz', '%b[]')" 0
     vendored: [bar]
     fr:       ERR malformed pattern (missing ']')   (interprets '[]' as a set)

   EVAL "return string.match('xx', '%bxx')" 0
     vendored: xx     (x is both open and close — degenerate but valid)
     fr:       (nil — fails to match)

   EVAL "return string.match('(unbal', '%b()')" 0
     vendored: (nil — no balanced match)
     fr:       5     (junk position from misinterpretation)

2. Capture validation: vendored raises at match-time on malformed
   captures. fr silently returns nil.

   EVAL "return string.match('abc', '(.*')" 0
     vendored: ERR unfinished capture
     fr:       (nil)

   EVAL "return string.match('abc', '.*)')" 0
     vendored: ERR invalid pattern capture
     fr:       (nil)

Fix locus: crates/fr-command/src/lua_eval.rs

- lua_pattern_validate / lua_pattern_element_len: recognize %b<open><close>
  as a 4-byte pattern element (do NOT fall through to '[set]' parsing
  when the byte after %b happens to be '[').
- lua_pat_match: add a %b branch (similar to the existing %f branch
  added in 3zxc1) that runs matchbalance: starting at the current
  string position, advance through paired open/close chars until depth
  hits 0, then continue matching at the resulting position.
- Pattern validate: track open-capture count and depth so that:
    * unfinished capture: '(' opened but no matching ')' raises
      "malformed pattern" / "unfinished capture" at the end of the
      pattern.
    * extra ')' raises "invalid pattern capture".
  Upstream uses two separate error wordings — pin both.

Test plan: pin the 6 cases above (3 balanced-match successes, 1 nil
balanced-match, 2 capture errors). Plus the existing %f / set /
quantifier regressions must continue to pass.
close_reason: Pattern engine in crates/fr-command/src/lua_eval.rs now supports two upstream features: (1) %bxy balanced-match — lua_pattern_validate and lua_pattern_element_len now recognize %b as a 4-byte element, and lua_pat_match got a %b branch ahead of the quantifier path that tracks open/close depth from the current string position (mirrors lstrlib.c::matchbalance, including the degenerate open==close case). Malformed %b lacking the 2-char suffix raises 'unbalanced pattern' verbatim. (2) Capture-balance validation — lua_pattern_validate now tracks open_captures count, raising 'unfinished capture' for unclosed '(' and 'invalid pattern capture' for extra ')'. Regression test lua_pattern_balanced_match_and_capture_validation_skwin pins 7 cases (5 balanced-match including nested and unbalanced; 2 capture errors). Live diff vs vendored Redis 7.2.4 on :16380 clean across 15 probes including gsub/find regressions.

title: SLOWLOG GET with extra args returns table-level wrong-arity instead of upstream subcommand-syntax envelope
description: Differential probe vs vendored 7.2.4: `SLOWLOG GET extra extra` (4 args) surfaces 'ERR unknown subcommand or wrong number of arguments for \'GET\'. Try SLOWLOG HELP.' — the subcommand-syntax-error envelope from addReplySubcommandSyntaxError (slowlog.c:165). fr's handler at fr-command/src/lib.rs:16062 returned WrongSubcommandArity which renders as 'wrong number of arguments for \'slowlog|get\' command' — distinct wire wording. title: [MOCK] Lua redis.set_repl is still a no-op stub

Current gap: Lua scripts can call redis.set_repl, but the evaluator drops the request instead of changing replication behavior, so the scripting surface still contains a placeholder API.

Real impl path: implement replication-mode state in crates/fr-command/src/lua_eval.rs near the redis.replicate_commands/redis.set_repl dispatch, then thread that state through the script execution path in crates/fr-runtime/src/lib.rs and downstream propagation in crates/fr-repl.

title: fr-command HRANDFIELD WITHVALUES / ZRANDMEMBER WITHSCORES missing tighter [-LONG_MAX/2, LONG_MAX/2] count range check

New helper require_count_fits_half_long_range checks count fits in [-i64::MAX/2, i64::MAX/2] and emits 'ERR value is out of range' (matching upstream's wording — no 'value must between' suffix on this specific guard). title: SPOP with extra args after count returns table-level wrong-arity instead of upstream syntax-error
description: Differential probe vs vendored Redis 7.2.4: `SPOP s 2 extra` returns `ERR syntax error`. Upstream commands.def declares SPOP with arity = -2 (so argc>=2 passes the table-level check), and t_set.c::spopCommand:974-977 maps `argc > 3` directly to `addReplyErrorObject(c, shared.syntaxerr)`. fr's spop handler had `argv.len() < 2 || argv.len() > 3 -> WrongArity`, so probes like `SPOP s 2 extra` and `SPOP s 2 a b` echoed `ERR wrong number of arguments for 'spop' command`. SRANDMEMBER (br-frankenredis-randextra) and HRANDFIELD already implement the same arity = -2 + handler-level syntax-error pattern.
acceptance_criteria: SPOP with argc < 2 must continue to return the table-level wrong-arity wording; argc in {2, 3} keeps existing behavior; argc > 3 must return `ERR syntax error` (matching upstream's syntaxerr branch).
close_reason: Split fr-command::spop's arity check so argc<2 still returns WrongArity but argc>3 returns CommandError::SyntaxError, matching upstream t_set.c::spopCommand. Pinned with spop_extra_args_after_count_use_handler_syntax_error_not_table_arity covering `SPOP s 2 extra` and `SPOP s 2 a b`. Existing 4 SPOP tests still green.

title: fr-store: flushdb leaks ordered_keys + hash_field_expires; KEYS/SCAN/RANDOMKEY return ghost keys after FLUSHALL
description:
Real correctness bug. After FLUSHALL/FLUSHDB:

  DBSIZE → 0          (ok)
  EXISTS k → 0         (ok, k was flushed)
  GET k    → nil       (ok)
  TYPE k   → none      (ok)
  KEYS *   → [list, weight_1, weight_2, weight_3]   ← ghost keys
  SCAN 0   → [list, weight_1, weight_2, weight_3]   ← ghost keys
  RANDOMKEY → "weight_3" (or other ghost)            ← ghost key

Differential probe vs vendored Redis 7.2.4: upstream RANDOMKEY returns
nil after FLUSHALL on an empty DB; fr returns a stale name from the
ordered_keys index.

Root cause at crates/fr-store/src/lib.rs:3743 — Store::flushdb clears
entries, stream_*, expires_count, db_*_counts, but NOT:

  ordered_keys              (BTreeSet<Vec<u8>>) — backs KEYS/SCAN/RANDOMKEY
  hash_field_expires        (BTreeMap<(Vec<u8>, Vec<u8>), u64>) — Redis 7.4 per-field TTLs

Same omission in flush_database (line 3776) for the BTreeSet — verify.

[CRITICAL] severity. KEYS/SCAN/RANDOMKEY are documented contracts; clients
that snapshot key inventories see stale data, and SCAN-based eviction
loops may infinite-loop on ghost cursors that never resolve.

Fix: Store::flushdb and Store::flush_database must clear both
ordered_keys (or scope-clear for the DB) and hash_field_expires.

Acceptance: after FLUSHALL, RANDOMKEY/KEYS *_/SCAN 0 all return empty;
existing flushdb_clears_all test extended.

Reference: /reality-check-for-project audit on 2026-05-04.
notes:
Fixed Store::flushdb at crates/fr-store/src/lib.rs:3743 to also clear ordered_keys (the BTreeSet backing KEYS/SCAN/RANDOMKEY) and hash_field_expires (the per-field TTL map for Redis 7.4 HEXPIRE family). Before: DBSIZE=0 / EXISTS=0 / GET=nil / TYPE=none, but KEYS/SCAN/RANDOMKEY surfaced ghost names. After: all enumeration surfaces consistent with the empty entries map.

flush_database (per-DB clear via FLUSHDB on a non-default DB) was already safe — it routes through internal_entries_remove which clears ordered_keys and hash_field_ttl_clear_for_key per key.

Added flushdb_clears_ordered_keys_and_hash_field_expires test that pre-populates ordered_keys via SET, hash_field_expires via direct map insert, then asserts all three (entries, ordered_keys, hash_field_expires) are empty post-flush AND that keys_matching('*') returns empty.

cargo test -p fr-store: 369 pass total; clippy clean.

Need: add a structure-aware fuzz target that drives multiple HLL keys through PFADD/PFCOUNT/PFMERGE sequences and asserts semantic invariants: repeated adds do not shrink counts, merges are idempotent, merging identical sources is stable, type errors do not mutate existing HLLs, and dump/restore preserves approximate counts within deterministic self-roundtrip bounds.

Real impl path: fuzz/Cargo.toml plus new fuzz/fuzz_targets/fuzz_store_hll.rs hitting the fr-store PF* APIs directly.

Need: add a structure-aware fuzz target that generates argv slices for these parser families, compares accepted outputs against normalization invariants, and asserts rejected inputs fail without panicking. For stream IDs and blocking timeouts, include boundary and canonicalization checks; for zstore/scan/geo parsers, verify option order and duplicate-option handling are stable.

title: CLIENT/SLOWLOG/PUBSUB/FUNCTION/ACL subcommand-syntax envelope hardcoded uppercase token
description: Differential probe vs vendored Redis 7.2.4: per-subcommand wrong-arity replies for non-DEBUG dispatched commands (CLIENT PAUSE, SLOWLOG GET, PUBSUB CHANNELS / SHARDCHANNELS, FUNCTION RESTORE / FLUSH, ACL GENPASS) are routed through addReplySubcommandSyntaxError, which echoes argv[1] verbatim (input case preserved). fr previously hard-coded each token in uppercase across both fr-command and fr-runtime dispatch paths, so lowercase / mixed-case input diverged from upstream byte-for-byte.
close_reason: Replaced 6 fr-command sites (FUNCTION RESTORE / FLUSH, CLIENT PAUSE, SLOWLOG GET, PUBSUB CHANNELS / SHARDCHANNELS) with format!("... '{sub}' ...") interpolation that preserves the input case. Replaced 4 fr-runtime sites: SLOWLOG GET / PUBSUB CHANNELS / PUBSUB SHARDCHANNELS now use format! with the runtime-local sub variable; ACL GENPASS reuses the existing acl_subcommand_syntax_error(argv) helper which already preserves case. Added an fr-command unit-test pin subcommand_arity_envelopes_preserve_input_case covering 10 mixed-case input shapes across all 6 subcommand families. fr-command 842/4 pre-existing stream failures, fr-runtime 337/1 pre-existing flaky bgsave test, fr-runtime admin 43/43.

title: SUBSCRIBE family hard-rejected inside MULTI; upstream queues them (no CMD_NO_MULTI flag)
description:
Upstream commands.def line 10755 tags subscribeCommand with CMD_PUBSUB|CMD_NOSCRIPT|CMD_LOADING|CMD_STALE|CMD_SENTINEL — there is no CMD_NO_MULTI flag, so server.c::processCommand:3920 lets SUBSCRIBE / UNSUBSCRIBE / PSUBSCRIBE / PUNSUBSCRIBE / SSUBSCRIBE / SUNSUBSCRIBE queue inside MULTI like any other command. fr was hard-rejecting the entire family with 'ERR Command not allowed inside a transaction'; vendored 7.2.4 returns QUEUED.

Repro vs vendored 7.2.4 (all 6 commands):
  16380> MULTI; SUBSCRIBE foo; DISCARD  →  OK; QUEUED; OK
  16381 (pre-fix)> MULTI; SUBSCRIBE foo; DISCARD  →  OK; ERR Command not allowed inside a transaction; OK

Fix: removed the special-case is_sub_cmd reject branch in the MULTI queueing path in fr-runtime. The existing CMD_NO_MULTI gate (frankenredis-exv69) correctly handles the genuine SAVE / SHUTDOWN / PSYNC cases via the is_no_multi_command bitmap, so SAVE in MULTI still rejects with EXECABORT and plain GET in MULTI still queues.
acceptance_criteria: 1. MULTI; SUBSCRIBE foo; DISCARD returns OK; QUEUED; OK (matches vendored). 2. Same for UNSUBSCRIBE, PSUBSCRIBE, PUNSUBSCRIBE, SSUBSCRIBE, SUNSUBSCRIBE. 3. MULTI; SAVE; ... still rejects SAVE with the no-multi error and triggers EXECABORT on EXEC. 4. MULTI; GET k; DISCARD still queues GET as before.

title: Add scan-family fuzz target for fr-store cursor and pattern iteration
description:
Evidence: fr-store exposes cursor-driven iterators at crates/fr-store/src/lib.rs:8421 (scan), 8471 (hscan), 8522 (sscan), and 8572 (zscan), but current fuzz coverage only hits glob matching and basic runtime sequences. There is no harness asserting cursor progress, duplicate suppression, or MATCH/COUNT stability across repeated scans.

Need: add a stateful/structure-aware fuzz target that seeds mixed keyspaces and collection contents, drives repeated SCAN/HSCAN/SSCAN/ZSCAN calls with fuzzed cursor/pattern/count inputs, and checks invariants: cursors eventually terminate, concatenated results are subsets of the live container, no item appears outside the selected type, and MATCH filtering agrees with the existing glob matcher.

Real impl path: fuzz/Cargo.toml plus new fuzz/fuzz_targets/fuzz_store_scan_family.rs targeting the fr-store scan APIs directly.
close_reason: Completed

title: fr-command SINTERCARD numkeys parse failure emits generic 'value is not an integer' instead of canonical 'numkeys should be greater than 0' wording
description:
## Symptom
\`SINTERCARD abc 2 s1 s2\` returns 'ERR value is not an integer or out of range' (CommandError::InvalidInteger). Upstream returns 'ERR numkeys should be greater than 0' for both parse failure AND out-of-range — same canonical wording.

## Differential probe
Upstream t_set.c::sinterCardCommand at legacy_redis_code/redis/src/t_set.c:1451-1453:
\`\`\`
if (getRangeLongFromObjectOrReply(c, c->argv[1], 1, LONG_MAX,
                                  &numkeys, \"numkeys should be greater than 0\") != C_OK)
    return;
\`\`\`

The msg argument 'numkeys should be greater than 0' is passed through getRangeLongFromObjectOrReply → getLongFromObjectOrReply → getLongLongFromObjectOrReply (object.c:895-936) which uses it for BOTH parse failure (non-integer input) AND out-of-range (< 1 or > LONG_MAX).

fr-command/src/lib.rs:11243-11248 currently uses \`parse_i64_arg(&argv[1])?\` which propagates CommandError::InvalidInteger before the bounds check fires — same family of defect as frankenredis-iglto (SLOWLOG GET) shipped in 3b816d5.

## Fix
Replace 'parse_i64_arg(&argv[1])?' + 'numkeys_val <= 0' bounds check with a closure-mapping pattern (analogous to the LIMIT branch already in this function at lines 11268-11278): any parse error or numkeys < 1 maps to RespFrame::Error('ERR numkeys should be greater than 0').

## Test
Add a new test sintercard_numkeys_validation_matches_redis_wording asserting four input variants — 'abc', empty string, '0', '-1' — all surface the canonical wording.

## Out of scope
Other commands using getRangeLongFromObjectOrReply with custom msg arguments are tracked separately (LMPOP/BLMPOP already correct, ZINTERCARD uses getLongFromObjectOrReply with NULL msg so the divergence doesn't apply).
close_reason: fr-command SINTERCARD numkeys parsing now mirrors upstream's single canonical 'numkeys should be greater than 0' wording for both parse failure (non-integer, empty) and out-of-range (< 1). Same closure-mapping pattern as the SLOWLOG GET fix (iglto, 3b816d5) and the existing LIMIT branch in this same function. New test sintercard_numkeys_validation_matches_redis_wording covers four input variants (abc, empty, 0, -1).

title: Lua: function as table key silently fails (rawset stores nothing, pairs errors)
description:
Vendored Redis 7.2.4 supports function values as table keys (Lua uses object identity). fr silently drops the key in rawset and errors with 'invalid key to next' when pairs encounters one.

Probe:
  EVAL "local t={}; local k=function() end; rawset(t, k, 'v'); return rawget(t, k)" 0
    vendored: v
    fr:       (empty / nil)

  EVAL "local t={}; local k=function() end; rawset(t, k, 'v'); local n=0; for kk,vv in pairs(t) do n=n+1 end; return n" 0
    vendored: 1
    fr:       ERR invalid key to 'next'

Root cause: fr's LuaTable backs hash keys with a structure that doesn't accept LuaValue::Function as a key (Functions don't implement Hash/Eq via pointer identity).

Impact: low — function-as-key is rare in real scripts. Same likely applies to thread, userdata key types. Filed as quality-parity gap.

Probe sweep #131.
close_reason: completed

title: Lua string.format: %e/%g with inf produces garbage, %x/%u with negatives
description:
Multiple divergences in fr's string.format for non-finite floats and negative integers vs vendored Redis 7.2.4:

1. %e / %g with infinity produces nonsense output:
   EVAL "return string.format('%e', 1/0)" 0
     vendored: inf
     fr:       NaNe+2147483647
   EVAL "return string.format('%g', 1/0)" 0
     vendored: inf
     fr:       NaNe+2147483647

   fr's scientific formatter does not special-case +/-inf or NaN, so
   the mantissa/exponent extraction produces garbage.

2. %x / %X / %o with negative numeric args returns 0:
   EVAL "return string.format('%x', -1)" 0
     vendored: ffffffffffffffff
     fr:       0
   EVAL "return string.format('%o', -1)" 0
     vendored: 1777777777777777777777
     fr:       0

   Rust's `-1.0_f64 as u64` saturates to 0; C's UB cast wraps to
   u64::MAX. Fix: cast via `as i64 as u64` to recover the two's
   complement bit pattern.

3. %u with negative arg returns signed value:
   EVAL "return string.format('%u', -1)" 0
     vendored: 18446744073709551615
     fr:       -1

   fr routes %u through the same i64-formatting branch as %d/%i;
   vendored interprets %u as unsigned and emits the bit pattern.
   Split %u into its own arm that casts via i64 -> u64.

4. %f with NaN: vendored emits "-nan", fr emits "NaN" — cosmetic only.

Test plan:
  - Pin %e/%g with 1/0 produce "inf" (and -1/0 produce "-inf").
  - Pin %x with -1 produces ffffffffffffffff (and -255 produces ffffffffffffff01).
  - Pin %u with -1 produces 18446744073709551615.
  - Pin %o with -1 produces 1777777777777777777777.
  - Pin valid finite values unchanged for all four specs.
close_reason: Lua string.format now matches vendored for non-finite floats (%e/%E/%f/%g/%G return inf/-inf/INF/-INF/-nan/-NAN) and negative inputs to unsigned conversions (%x/%X/%o/%u recover two's complement bit pattern). Added lua_fmt_nonfinite helper; %u split from %d arm to cast via i64->u64; %x/%X/%o cast via i64->u64. Regression test string_format_inf_nan_and_neg_unsigned_t1ah8 in crates/fr-command/src/lua_eval.rs covers 24 cases. Live diff vs vendored Redis 7.2.4 on :16380 clean for all probed inputs.

title: cjson.encode emits integers as plain decimal and skips slash escape; drifts from upstream lua_cjson
description:
fr's cjson.encode has three divergences from vendored Redis 7.2.4's bundled lua_cjson:

1. **-0 collapses to 0**: cjson.encode(-0) returns "0" on fr, "-0" on vendored. fr's integer-cast path treats -0.0 as 0 because (*n as i64) as f64 == n is true for -0.0.

2. **Large integer-valued floats emit too many digits**: cjson.encode(2^53) returns "9007199254740992" on fr, "9.007199254741e+15" on vendored. fr's integer-cast path bypasses the upstream %.14g rounding. Same for 2^53+1.

3. **Forward slash not escaped**: cjson.encode('hello /world') returns "hello /world" on fr, "hello \/world" on vendored. JSON allows but doesn't require / escaping; lua_cjson always escapes it (for HTML/script-safe embedding).

Root cause:
- For (1) and (2): the encoder at fr-command/src/lua_eval.rs:9357 special-cases integer-valued numbers and emits via format!("{}", *n as i64). Upstream lua_cjson always routes through fpconv_g_fmt which is essentially %.14g — fr already has lua_number_to_string() (line 9193) that implements %.14g correctly, including the -0 sign preservation at lines 9209-9219.
- For (3): json_escape_bytes (line 9168) handles double-quote, backslash, control chars, but not /.

Probe sweep #67 (probe137.sh):
  vendored cjson.encode(-0)    -> -0    fr -> 0
  vendored cjson.encode(2^53)  -> 9.007199254741e+15    fr -> 9007199254740992
  vendored cjson.encode(2^53+1)-> 9.007199254741e+15    fr -> 9007199254740992
  vendored cjson.encode('/h')  -> "\/h"    fr -> "/h"

Fix plan:
- Route LuaValue::Number through lua_number_to_string() in lua_value_to_json. That fixes both -0 and large-integer cases (already %.14g-formatted).
- Add / escape branch to json_escape_bytes.
- Regression test pins all three cases against the upstream wording.
close_reason: Fixed in iteration 101: Routed cjson.encode's LuaValue::Number arm through lua_number_to_string (the existing %.14g-faithful formatter), and added the '/' -> '\\/' escape branch to json_escape_bytes. Three behaviors aligned with upstream lua_cjson: (a) -0 preserves sign, (b) 2^53 and 2^53+1 emit '9.007199254741e+15' via %.14g rounding, (c) forward slash escapes in strings. Sanity-preserved: small integers and finite floats still emit the same way. Live-diff: 7/7 cases match vendored. Regression test cjson_encode_matches_upstream_number_and_slash_t6bqz pins -0, 2^53, 2^53+1, small int (1, 123), single-slash, double-slash. Full 1025-test suite passes.

title: COMMAND INFO ZRANGESTORE/GEOSEARCHSTORE use wrong find_keys type (keynum vs range)
description: fr-command/src/lib.rs:15125 lumps ZRANGESTORE and GEOSEARCHSTORE with ZINTERSTORE/ZUNIONSTORE/ZDIFFSTORE, all emitting a 'keynum' find_keys type for the source. But upstream commands.def declares ZRANGESTORE and GEOSEARCHSTORE with 2 simple index keyspecs (dst@1 + src@2, both KSPEC_BS_INDEX + KSPEC_FK_RANGE {lastkey=0,keystep=1,limit=0}) -- there is no keynum; only one source key. ZINTERSTORE/ZUNIONSTORE/ZDIFFSTORE genuinely take keynum + N sources. Fix: split ZRANGESTORE/GEOSEARCHSTORE into a separate match arm using two make_keyspec_index_range(&['OW','update']/['RO','access'], 1/2, 0, 1) entries. Acceptance: both byte-exact with vendored.

Split them out of the existing zinterstore/zunionstore/zdiffstore arm (which legitimately uses keynum for the multi-source numkeys + N srcs pattern) into their own match arm at fr-command/src/lib.rs:15128, using two make_keyspec_index_range calls. Live differential probe: ZRANGESTORE/GEOSEARCHSTORE now byte-exact; ZINTERSTORE/ZUNIONSTORE/ZDIFFSTORE remain byte-exact (still in the keynum arm). Tests: fr-command 910 pass.

title: COMMAND GETKEYS OBJECT <subcmd> k returns subcommand name instead of key
description:
Differential probe vs vendored redis 7.2.4:

  COMMAND GETKEYS OBJECT ENCODING k    → v=k, fr=ENCODING   ← bug
  COMMAND GETKEYS OBJECT REFCOUNT k    → v=k, fr=REFCOUNT   ← bug
  COMMAND GETKEYS OBJECT IDLETIME k    → v=k, fr=IDLETIME   ← bug
  COMMAND GETKEYS OBJECT FREQ k        → v=k, fr=FREQ       ← bug
  COMMAND GETKEYS OBJECT HELP          → v=ERR no key args, fr=HELP ← bug
  COMMAND GETKEYS OBJECT               → v=ERR no key args, fr=Invalid number of arguments ← also slightly wrong (but acceptable)

COMMAND GETKEYSANDFLAGS exhibits the same key-extraction bug.

Root cause: crates/fr-command/src/lib.rs:1033-1068 lumps OBJECT in
with LLEN/HLEN/SCARD/etc. (metadata-only readonly ops) and returns
`vec![CommandKeyReference { index: 1, flags: KEY_FLAGS_RO }]`. But
OBJECT is a subcommand-style parent — the key is at argv[2], not
argv[1]. argv[1] is the subcommand name (ENCODING, REFCOUNT, etc.).

Note: command_key_indexes() at line 346 correctly returns vec![2]
for OBJECT, but command_key_references_with_exact_flags is called
first (line 732) and short-circuits to the wrong index=1.

Fix: remove OBJECT from the LLEN/HLEN list. Add a dedicated OBJECT
branch that dispatches on argv[1] (the subcommand):
  - ENCODING/REFCOUNT/IDLETIME/FREQ → vec![{index:2, flags:RO}]
  - HELP → Err(NoKeyArguments)
  - unknown sub or short argv → fall through to parent arity/keyspec
    handling so existing error wording survives.

Also covers GETKEYSANDFLAGS since it shares the same code path.
close_reason: Added subcommand_parent_key_references() pre-arity hook for OBJECT. Removed OBJECT from LLEN/HLEN exact-flags block. Now: OBJECT ENCODING/REFCOUNT/IDLETIME/FREQ k → key at argv[2] with RO flag; OBJECT HELP → no key args; OBJECT BOGUS → Invalid command specified; bare OBJECT → no key args; OBJECT ENCODING (missing key) → Invalid number of arguments. All 8 OBJECT variants match vendored 7.2.4, no regression in non-OBJECT GETKEYS.

title: Lua table assignment silently accepts nil/NaN keys
description:
Lua 5.1 raises a runtime error when assigning to a table with a nil or NaN key:

  EVAL "local t={} t[nil]=1" 0
    vendored: ERR user_script:1: table index is nil
    fr:       (silently succeeds — value lost)

  EVAL "local t={} t[0/0]=1" 0
    vendored: ERR user_script:1: table index is NaN
    fr:       (silently succeeds — value lost)

  EVAL "local t={} t[1/0]=1" 0     -- positive infinity is allowed
    vendored: ok
    fr:       ok

Reproduction (probed against vendored Redis 7.2.4 on :16380 / fr on :16480):
- t[nil] = 1   → 'table index is nil'
- t[0/0] = 1   → 'table index is NaN'
- t[-0/0] = 1  → 'table index is NaN'
- t[1/0] = 1   → ok (infinity is fine, only NaN is rejected)

Upstream implementation: lvm.c::luaV_settable / ltable.c::luaH_set raise
luaL_error("table index is %s") with "nil" or "NaN" depending on the key.
The check fires in newkey() before the slot is allocated, so the value
is never stored.

fr's table implementation silently swallows the assignment, which means
real scripts that use a divisor-of-zero or undefined value as a key see
their data quietly disappear — a worst-case parity gap because it
corrupts state without raising.

Fix locus: crates/fr-command/src/lua_eval.rs — the LuaTable set path
(likely LuaTable::set or wherever table-assignment opcodes dispatch).
Add a guard: if key is LuaValue::Nil, raise "user_script:1: table index is nil".
If key is LuaValue::Number(n) and n.is_nan(), raise "user_script:1: table index is NaN".

Test plan: pin all 4 cases above against pcall to capture the error string,
plus the positive-infinity success case.
close_reason: Added lua_check_table_key helper in crates/fr-command/src/lua_eval.rs which raises 'user_script:1: table index is nil' on nil keys and 'user_script:1: table index is NaN' on NaN keys. Wired into both user-visible assignment sites: Expr::Index in Stmt::Assign (t[k]=v syntax) and TableField::Index in TableConstructor ({[k]=v}). Positive/negative infinity remain valid keys. rawset's pre-existing nil/NaN check (no prefix, matching its C-level error origin) is unchanged. Regression test lua_table_rejects_nil_and_nan_keys_tb9vb pins 7 cases. Live diff vs vendored Redis 7.2.4 on :16380 clean across 13 probes.

title: Lua string-library indexing missing: s.fld on string should return nil, not error
description: fr's Expr::Field errors with 'attempt to index a string value' when reading a field from a string (e.g. local s='abc'; s.fld). Vendored Lua 5.1 attaches the 'string' library as the metatable __index for strings, so unknown string fields return nil (and known ones like s:upper return the function). Probe: `local s='abc'; s.fld()` on vendored emits 'attempt to call field \'fld\' (a nil value)' while fr emits 'attempt to index a string value'. Implementation: in Expr::Field eval, if the value is a string and the field is not a string-library entry, return Nil instead of erroring; if it IS a string-library entry, return that LuaValue::RustFunction so s:upper() etc. work. Discovered while testing frankenredis-md71j call-error wording.
close_reason: Implemented string-as-metatable __index in lua_eval.rs: Expr::Field, Expr::Index, and both Expr::MethodCall arms (single + trailing-arg expansion) now call new lookup_string_field() helper for LuaValue::Str receivers. The helper reads from globals['string'] table, returning the relevant RustFunction for known fields (upper/lower/sub/len/etc.) or Nil for unknown fields. Also fixed lua_raw_equal to compare RustFunctions by name so 'string.upper == s.upper' is true (vendored treats them as identical). Verified live diff vs vendored 7.2.4 (port 16380): 18/18 cases match including s.fld (nil), s.upper (function), s:upper()/s:len()/s:sub()/s:reverse()/s:find()/s:byte()/s:rep()/s:format()/s:gsub() calls, s['upper'](s), numeric s[1] returning nil, and the 'attempt to call field fld' error after the field resolves to nil. Pinned by lua_string_indexing_routes_through_string_library covering 8 cases plus 3 negative receiver-type checks. All 913 fr-command lib tests pass.

title: Align fr-command CLIENT TRACKING/CACHING replies with Redis/runtime semantics
